On read: (1) recompute the inscription source/provenance witness (branching primary vs derived); (2) re-check the inscription credibility cap; (3) verify append-only log integrity under A1; (4) re-verify all eleven admissibility conditions for every event; (5) check replay equality of the fold against the stored chi_t. The groups are deliberately separable so each threat model is individually observable.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
five-group record-level audit replay on read: (1) recompute the inscription source/provenance witness (branching primary vs derived); (2) re-check the inscription credibility cap; (3) verify append-only log integrity under a1; (4) re-verify all eleven admissibility conditions for every event; (5) check replay equality of the fold against the stored chi_t. the groups are deliberately separable so each threat model is individually observable. mechanism papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Two audits at two times: record replay vs route audit

mechanism

Record-level replay runs on every read against the registry active at each event's timestamp; routing-policy audit runs once per route at registration. Conflating them into one pass would either fail in registries with many routes or silently skip the routing check on records read against a stale registry view.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
two audits at two times: record replay vs route audit record-level replay runs on every read against the registry active at each event's timestamp; routing-policy audit runs once per route at registration. conflating them into one pass would either fail in registries with many routes or silently skip the routing check on records read against a stale registry view. mechanism papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ "Specification invariant" as an honest naming convention

framing/ontology

The soundness results (record-replay soundness, routing isolation, combined soundness) are deliberately named specification invariants, not theorems: each is a direct corollary of the stated contracts ("the specified contracts entail these invariants by construction"), not a preservation proof over an adversarial transition system. The gap between the two is named, not hidden.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
specification invariant as an honest naming convention the soundness results (record-replay soundness, routing isolation, combined soundness) are deliberately named specification invariants, not theorems: each is a direct corollary of the stated contracts ( the specified contracts entail these invariants by construction ), not a preservation proof over an adversarial transition system. the gap between the two is named, not hidden. framing/ontology papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Collapse to source downgrading when logs are empty

result/finding

If every record's credibility log stays empty forever, the credibility-layered model reduces exactly to source downgrading with a static chi_0 annotation — a formal backward-compatibility proof that the extension adds nothing incompatible.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
collapse to source downgrading when logs are empty if every record's credibility log stays empty forever, the credibility-layered model reduces exactly to source downgrading with a static chi_0 annotation — a formal backward-compatibility proof that the extension adds nothing incompatible. result/finding papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Drift-bounded credibility (per-event rule caps)

mechanism

Every accepted event satisfies |chi^+ − chi^-| ≤ Δ_max of its own rule; different rules carry different caps. Bounds the per-event velocity of belief change and makes a runaway confirmation cascade impossible in one step.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
drift-bounded credibility (per-event rule caps) every accepted event satisfies |chi^+ − chi^-| ≤ δ_max of its own rule; different rules carry different caps. bounds the per-event velocity of belief change and makes a runaway confirmation cascade impossible in one step. mechanism papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ C4: reactivation-induced provenance overlap (corrupted-store anomaly)

experiment/benchmark

The derivation DAG is acyclic by construction, but reactivation or corrupted-store imports can produce two records whose stored provenance sets each list the other through a multi-hop reactivation path. The fixture treats this as a corrupted-store anomaly (not a true cycle) and shows the same bidirectional intersection test rejects confirmations in both directions.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
c4: reactivation-induced provenance overlap (corrupted-store anomaly) the derivation dag is acyclic by construction, but reactivation or corrupted-store imports can produce two records whose stored provenance sets each list the other through a multi-hop reactivation path. the fixture treats this as a corrupted-store anomaly (not a true cycle) and shows the same bidirectional intersection test rejects confirmations in both directions. experiment/benchmark papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Nine-policy negative-control design

experiment/benchmark

The proposed rule is compared against seven negative controls plus one isolating control, each a complete implementation differing only in one disabled check: source-promotion-on-confirmation, credibility-as-source, credibility-only source path, average-cap inscription, closure-permissive updates, mutable log, unanchored update, and attestation-only.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
nine-policy negative-control design the proposed rule is compared against seven negative controls plus one isolating control, each a complete implementation differing only in one disabled check: source-promotion-on-confirmation, credibility-as-source, credibility-only source path, average-cap inscription, closure-permissive updates, mutable log, unanchored update, and attestation-only. experiment/benchmark papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Average-cap inscription = confidence laundering at inscription

dead-end

Setting chi_0 to the contributor mean instead of the contributor minimum is itself a laundering pattern (a weak contributor is averaged away). Surfaced in the fixture as exactly one inscription-cap violation.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
average-cap inscription = confidence laundering at inscription setting chi_0 to the contributor mean instead of the contributor minimum is itself a laundering pattern (a weak contributor is averaged away). surfaced in the fixture as exactly one inscription-cap violation. dead-end papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Attestation-only control: attestation ≠ independence

result/finding

A control that verifies evidence attestation tokens but skips closure independence passes attestation and still fails self-confirmation — isolating the claim that evidence-channel attestation is insufficient without provenance-closure independence. Cryptographic verification of who supplied evidence says nothing about whether the evidence is independent.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
attestation-only control: attestation ≠ independence a control that verifies evidence attestation tokens but skips closure independence passes attestation and still fails self-confirmation — isolating the claim that evidence-channel attestation is insufficient without provenance-closure independence. cryptographic verification of who supplied evidence says nothing about whether the evidence is independent. result/finding papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Deterministic conformance numbers (TVC)

result/finding

Over 33 audited records per policy: proposed rule zero violations across twelve check columns; source_promotion 2 source violations; each invalid routing control affects 32 accepted records; average_cap 1 cap violation; closure_permissive and attestation_only 3 closure + 3 source-class violations each; mutable_log 1 replay violation; unanchored_update 7 anchor/attestation/replay violations. The C6 tamper case yields exactly one audit rejection for the proposed rule — the expected detection, not a violation.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
deterministic conformance numbers (tvc) over 33 audited records per policy: proposed rule zero violations across twelve check columns; source_promotion 2 source violations; each invalid routing control affects 32 accepted records; average_cap 1 cap violation; closure_permissive and attestation_only 3 closure + 3 source-class violations each; mutable_log 1 replay violation; unanchored_update 7 anchor/attestation/replay violations. the c6 tamper case yields exactly one audit rejection for the proposed rule — the expected detection, not a violation. result/finding papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Randomized update-DAG harness with independent oracle

result/finding

500 trials / seeds 0–499; 5 base + 8 derived records and 12 candidate events per trial; 6,000 candidates realized as 1,684 admissible / 4,316 inadmissible; zero false rejections, zero false admissions, zero accepted-record audit violations across 11,271 audited records. The admissibility oracle is implemented independently of the policy under test. Event-type counts: 751 self/ancestor attempts (483 self, 268 ancestor-side; 193 nominally in-closure attempts landed admissible because the receiving record had empty provenance), 514 descendant, 510 shared-provenance, 505 direction-mismatch, 494 unregistered-rule.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex; docs\TIME_VARYING_CREDIBILITY_DELTA.md
randomized update-dag harness with independent oracle 500 trials / seeds 0–499; 5 base + 8 derived records and 12 candidate events per trial; 6,000 candidates realized as 1,684 admissible / 4,316 inadmissible; zero false rejections, zero false admissions, zero accepted-record audit violations across 11,271 audited records. the admissibility oracle is implemented independently of the policy under test. event-type counts: 751 self/ancestor attempts (483 self, 268 ancestor-side; 193 nominally in-closure attempts landed admissible because the receiving record had empty provenance), 514 descendant, 510 shared-provenance, 505 direction-mismatch, 494 unregistered-rule. result/finding papers\time_varying_credibility.tex; docs\time_varying_credibility_delta.md

◈ Eleven-mutant guard-removal catalog, 11/11 detected

experiment/benchmark

Seeded mutation testing disables one guard long enough to commit an unsafe event, then replays under strict audit: ancestor/descendant/shared-provenance closure, source admissibility, direction, policy registry, append-only, prefix rewrite, route source guard, evidence witness, attestation verification. Detection rate 1.0; the pytest guard requires the full catalog to be detected.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex; docs\TIME_VARYING_CREDIBILITY_DELTA.md
eleven-mutant guard-removal catalog, 11/11 detected seeded mutation testing disables one guard long enough to commit an unsafe event, then replays under strict audit: ancestor/descendant/shared-provenance closure, source admissibility, direction, policy registry, append-only, prefix rewrite, route source guard, evidence witness, attestation verification. detection rate 1.0; the pytest guard requires the full catalog to be detected. experiment/benchmark papers\time_varying_credibility.tex; docs\time_varying_credibility_delta.md

◈ Coverage matrix: formal condition → deterministic case → generator type → mutant → JSON field

experiment/benchmark

A traceability table mapping each of the eleven formal admissibility conditions plus routing to the deterministic case that exercises it, the randomized event type, the mutation probe, and the exact JSON output field — an auditable claim-to-evidence lattice for the whole paper.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
coverage matrix: formal condition → deterministic case → generator type → mutant → json field a traceability table mapping each of the eleven formal admissibility conditions plus routing to the deterministic case that exercises it, the randomized event type, the mutation probe, and the exact json output field — an auditable claim-to-evidence lattice for the whole paper. experiment/benchmark papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Five-step derivation walkthrough with routing crossover

metaphor

E (ext, 0.90) and T (tool, 0.80) derive c1 with chi_0 ≤ min(0.90, 0.80, β_chi(der)=0.70) = 0.70; three admissible external confirmations move c5's credibility 0.70→0.80→0.90→1.00 under drift cap 0.15. A forensic route (src_req ⪰ tool, chi ≥ 0.5) admits E but rejects c5 despite chi_t(c5)=1.00 > chi_t(E); a planning route (src_req ⪰ infer, chi ≥ 0.8) admits c5.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
five-step derivation walkthrough with routing crossover e (ext, 0.90) and t (tool, 0.80) derive c1 with chi_0 ≤ min(0.90, 0.80, β_chi(der)=0.70) = 0.70; three admissible external confirmations move c5's credibility 0.70→0.80→0.90→1.00 under drift cap 0.15. a forensic route (src_req ⪰ tool, chi ≥ 0.5) admits e but rejects c5 despite chi_t(c5)=1.00 > chi_t(e); a planning route (src_req ⪰ infer, chi ≥ 0.8) admits c5. metaphor papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ TVC limitations as idea seeds

open-question

(1) No calibrated probabilities — a separate calibration layer must consume chi_t. (2) Over-suppression is not prevented: a correct observation contradicted by noisy reactivation chains will drift down; whether that is warranted is the kernel's design problem, not the inscription rule's. (3) Kernel correctness is not adjudicated — audit detects hash mismatch, not which kernel is right. (4) No cross-agent transport (credibility dies at API boundaries without envelopes). (5) Non-zero audit latency on every read; sampled/cached replay possible but must itself be integrity-checked.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
tvc limitations as idea seeds (1) no calibrated probabilities — a separate calibration layer must consume chi_t. (2) over-suppression is not prevented: a correct observation contradicted by noisy reactivation chains will drift down; whether that is warranted is the kernel's design problem, not the inscription rule's. (3) kernel correctness is not adjudicated — audit detects hash mismatch, not which kernel is right. (4) no cross-agent transport (credibility dies at api boundaries without envelopes). (5) non-zero audit latency on every read; sampled/cached replay possible but must itself be integrity-checked. open-question papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Cost model incl. the unsafe-truncation warning

mechanism

Update is O(min(p_r, p_e)) expected (one hash-set intersection dominates); replay is O(ℓ_r) kernel/registry/hash operations; routing-policy audit is paid once per route. Log storage grows linearly with events, and truncation policies dropping old events are declared unsafe without an attested rollup of the dropped prefix — a named design constraint for compaction.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
cost model incl. the unsafe-truncation warning update is o(min(p_r, p_e)) expected (one hash-set intersection dominates); replay is o(ℓ_r) kernel/registry/hash operations; routing-policy audit is paid once per route. log storage grows linearly with events, and truncation policies dropping old events are declared unsafe without an attested rollup of the dropped prefix — a named design constraint for compaction. mechanism papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Future work: confidence layer with calibration-channel attestation

open-question

Apply the same append-only log discipline to the third quantity (confidence), with attestation tied to a calibration channel rather than an evidence channel.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
future work: confidence layer with calibration-channel attestation apply the same append-only log discipline to the third quantity (confidence), with attestation tied to a calibration channel rather than an evidence channel. open-question papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Future work: downstream calibration of chi_t

open-question

Platt scaling, isotonic regression, or temperature scaling applied downstream of the credibility layer, provided the calibration mapping is itself registered and audited — calibration must enter the same audit regime, not sit outside it.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
future work: downstream calibration of chi_t platt scaling, isotonic regression, or temperature scaling applied downstream of the credibility layer, provided the calibration mapping is itself registered and audited — calibration must enter the same audit regime, not sit outside it. open-question papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Future work: adversarial confirmation streams

open-question

Feed the layer confirmation events that satisfy every syntactic admissibility condition but are content-correlated in ways the kernel cannot detect. Expected: the credibility layer admits them and routing rejects some via source class; the empirical question is what fraction is caught at routing versus requiring content-level defenses.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
future work: adversarial confirmation streams feed the layer confirmation events that satisfy every syntactic admissibility condition but are content-correlated in ways the kernel cannot detect. expected: the credibility layer admits them and routing rejects some via source class; the empirical question is what fraction is caught at routing versus requiring content-level defenses. open-question papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Future work: operational semantics and preservation proofs (TVC)

open-question

Define a transition system over (record store, policy registry, authorization log) with inscription/update/audit/routing/adversary transitions and prove preservation over arbitrary sequences — upgrading specification-level corollaries to preservation theorems and making the guarantees "feel less tautological."

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
future work: operational semantics and preservation proofs (tvc) define a transition system over (record store, policy registry, authorization log) with inscription/update/audit/routing/adversary transitions and prove preservation over arbitrary sequences — upgrading specification-level corollaries to preservation theorems and making the guarantees feel less tautological. open-question papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Future work: cross-agent transport of the credibility log

open-question

The log requires either mirroring with signed commits or summarization with an attested summary hash to survive agent boundaries — explicitly framed as inheriting the same solution shape as label transport.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
future work: cross-agent transport of the credibility log the log requires either mirroring with signed commits or summarization with an attested summary hash to survive agent boundaries — explicitly framed as inheriting the same solution shape as label transport. open-question papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Related-work positioning: AGM / TMS / default logic / provenance

framing/ontology

Distinctions drawn: (a) "belief revision" is used in the engineering sense — no AGM postulates for content, the contribution is the inscription/audit contract preventing revision from rewriting source; (b) vs truth-maintenance systems, the credibility log is narrower (evidence-paired scalar moves for one record, not a global dependency network) — the shared piece is audit use of reasons; (c) vs nonmonotonic/default logic, the nonmonotonic object is chi_t, never Source; (d) vs provenance semirings and confidence propagation, complementary: confidence may move as an annotation while the support relation stays auditable.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
related-work positioning: agm / tms / default logic / provenance distinctions drawn: (a) belief revision is used in the engineering sense — no agm postulates for content, the contribution is the inscription/audit contract preventing revision from rewriting source; (b) vs truth-maintenance systems, the credibility log is narrower (evidence-paired scalar moves for one record, not a global dependency network) — the shared piece is audit use of reasons; (c) vs nonmonotonic/default logic, the nonmonotonic object is chi_t, never source; (d) vs provenance semirings and confidence propagation, complementary: confidence may move as an annotation while the support relation stays auditable. framing/ontology papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ TVC compatibility sketches with the other layers

framing/ontology

Interface sketches (explicitly not evidence claims): per-record valuation — carry both an integrity scalar and chi_0, both contributor-min-bounded, one monotone one revisable; context-dependent valuation — pin the credibility kernel under the same task-authorized context so one witness binds both layers; label transport — the credibility log needs signed envelopes exactly as the source label does.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
tvc compatibility sketches with the other layers interface sketches (explicitly not evidence claims): per-record valuation — carry both an integrity scalar and chi_0, both contributor-min-bounded, one monotone one revisable; context-dependent valuation — pin the credibility kernel under the same task-authorized context so one witness binds both layers; label transport — the credibility log needs signed envelopes exactly as the source label does. framing/ontology papers\time_varying_credibility.tex

◈ Delta report as a claim-state ledger (planned → validated)

experiment/benchmark

The delta doc tracks every claim's move from planned to validated with pointing evidence: RouteSpec + audit_routing_policy replaced predicate-only routing; EvidenceTemporalWitness added into event hashes; attestation upgraded from channel-hash equality to a verification relation over (channel, evidence, witness, timestamp, token); Assumption A1 added; bidirectional closure moved from spec-only to harness-validated; prefix-rewrite probes with per-record trusted log heads added. A "future work collapsed into main body" table records which promises were discharged.

docs\TIME_VARYING_CREDIBILITY_DELTA.md
delta report as a claim-state ledger (planned → validated) the delta doc tracks every claim's move from planned to validated with pointing evidence: routespec + audit_routing_policy replaced predicate-only routing; evidencetemporalwitness added into event hashes; attestation upgraded from channel-hash equality to a verification relation over (channel, evidence, witness, timestamp, token); assumption a1 added; bidirectional closure moved from spec-only to harness-validated; prefix-rewrite probes with per-record trusted log heads added. a future work collapsed into main body table records which promises were discharged. experiment/benchmark docs\time_varying_credibility_delta.md

◈ Named release blockers for arXiv v1

product

The delta doc lists concrete release blockers: no public origin remote at the time, no release commit pinning the current edits, no archived snapshot DOI — release engineering treated as a first-class checklist item alongside formal claims.

docs\TIME_VARYING_CREDIBILITY_DELTA.md
named release blockers for arxiv v1 the delta doc lists concrete release blockers: no public origin remote at the time, no release commit pinning the current edits, no archived snapshot doi — release engineering treated as a first-class checklist item alongside formal claims. product docs\time_varying_credibility_delta.md

◈ Reconsolidation citations as the biological shadow of revisable belief

metaphor

The bibliography carries Nader et al. 2000 and Schiller et al. 2010 (memory reconsolidation) alongside source monitoring — the biological precedent that retrieval reopens belief while the design intuition note insists the analogy carries no evidential weight.

papers\Time_Varying_Credibility.tex
---
reconsolidation citations as the biological shadow of revisable belief the bibliography carries nader et al. 2000 and schiller et al. 2010 (memory reconsolidation) alongside source monitoring — the biological precedent that retrieval reopens belief while the design intuition note insists the analogy carries no evidential weight. metaphor papers\time_varying_credibility.tex ---

Context-Dependent Valuation (CDV)

◈ Context-dependent integrity valuation (the core mechanism)

mechanism

Each valuation context κ (perception, simulation planning, forensic audit, …) supplies its own source order and class-interval policy; records carry context-indexed scalars tau_kappa(r). A derived write is scored under exactly one task-authorized context, capped by weakest contributor and operation ceiling under that context, and inscribed with the context, its policy hash, and the contributor witness.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
"context may change valuation, but inscription must bind valuation to its task-authorized context."
context-dependent integrity valuation (the core mechanism) each valuation context κ (perception, simulation planning, forensic audit, …) supplies its own source order and class-interval policy; records carry context-indexed scalars tau_kappa(r). a derived write is scored under exactly one task-authorized context, capped by weakest contributor and operation ceiling under that context, and inscribed with the context, its policy hash, and the contributor witness. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex context may change valuation, but inscription must bind valuation to its task-authorized context.

◈ Context laundering (coinage)

coinage

Scoring a record under a permissive context, writing it without its context, and later routing it under a stricter context as if the stricter context had authorized the score — the context analogue of inference laundering and confidence laundering. The failure is not that the permissive context exists; it is that the inscription does not bind the score to the context that produced it.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
context laundering (coinage) scoring a record under a permissive context, writing it without its context, and later routing it under a stricter context as if the stricter context had authorized the score — the context analogue of inference laundering and confidence laundering. the failure is not that the permissive context exists; it is that the inscription does not bind the score to the context that produced it. coinage papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The triple-equality invariant kappa_score = kappa_rec = kappa_star

mechanism

Three context values are in play: the context used to compute the score, the context written into metadata, and the task-authorized context. A valid inscription requires all three equal; the three laundering baselines correspond to breaking distinct equalities (implicit context omits kappa_rec; context mismatch breaks score=rec; adversarial selection breaks rec=star while keeping score=rec).

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the triple-equality invariant kappa_score = kappa_rec = kappa_star three context values are in play: the context used to compute the score, the context written into metadata, and the task-authorized context. a valid inscription requires all three equal; the three laundering baselines correspond to breaking distinct equalities (implicit context omits kappa_rec; context mismatch breaks score=rec; adversarial selection breaks rec=star while keeping score=rec). mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Impossibility proposition: a single global order cannot exist

result/finding

If two contexts order a class pair oppositely (perception: sim low; planner: sim above react/infer), no single global total order can agree with both — trivial mathematically, "load-bearing rhetorically," because it forces context-indexing rather than making it a convenience.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
"The proposition is trivial mathematically but load-bearing rhetorically"
impossibility proposition: a single global order cannot exist if two contexts order a class pair oppositely (perception: sim low; planner: sim above react/infer), no single global total order can agree with both — trivial mathematically, load-bearing rhetorically, because it forces context-indexing rather than making it a convenience. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex the proposition is trivial mathematically but load-bearing rhetorically

◈ Same-record dual valuation (S at 0.22 and 0.72)

metaphor

The same simulated branch S has tau_perc(S)=0.22 (weak perception evidence) and tau_plan(S)=0.72 (first-class planning artifact). Both values are legitimate in their contexts; the unsafe object is a planner-scored value written without the fact that planning, rather than perception, authorized it.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
same-record dual valuation (s at 0.22 and 0.72) the same simulated branch s has tau_perc(s)=0.22 (weak perception evidence) and tau_plan(s)=0.72 (first-class planning artifact). both values are legitimate in their contexts; the unsafe object is a planner-scored value written without the fact that planning, rather than perception, authorized it. metaphor papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Context changes audit expectations, not just rankings

framing/ontology

Forensic audit may require stricter intervals for every internally produced class: a reactivated memory can be adequate for planning while too weak for a forensic conclusion. Context indexes both the order and the interval widths.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
context changes audit expectations, not just rankings forensic audit may require stricter intervals for every internally produced class: a reactivated memory can be adequate for planning while too weak for a forensic conclusion. context indexes both the order and the interval widths. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ RAG fetches are not reactivation

framing/ontology

A taxonomic clarification: in the source-downgrading lattice, react denotes a prior record surfaced from the agent's own store; external RAG-style fetches are handled by ingestion, tool-output, or external-observation labels — heading off a natural misreading of the lattice.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
rag fetches are not reactivation a taxonomic clarification: in the source-downgrading lattice, react denotes a prior record surfaced from the agent's own store; external rag-style fetches are handled by ingestion, tool-output, or external-observation labels — heading off a natural misreading of the lattice. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Valuation context as policy object (order + intervals + ceilings)

mechanism

A context contains a total source order, per-class interval policy I_kappa(s)=[l,u], and operation ceiling classes. The operation-to-ceiling-class mapping γ is global in the artifact but a context-dependent γ_kappa variant is explicitly possible since the policy hash already commits to the mapping.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
valuation context as policy object (order + intervals + ceilings) a context contains a total source order, per-class interval policy i_kappa(s)=[l,u], and operation ceiling classes. the operation-to-ceiling-class mapping γ is global in the artifact but a context-dependent γ_kappa variant is explicitly possible since the policy hash already commits to the mapping. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Context-consistent interval policies as ordered covers of [0,1]

mechanism

Intervals must form an ordered cover: lowest class begins at 0, highest ends at 1, adjacent classes share the boundary value (u_a = l_b). Validated for every configured context before the valuation object can be used.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
context-consistent interval policies as ordered covers of [0,1] intervals must form an ordered cover: lowest class begins at 0, highest ends at 1, adjacent classes share the boundary value (u_a = l_b). validated for every configured context before the valuation object can be used. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The authorized task context is an input, never an output

mechanism

kappa_star is selected by the task controller / scheduler / supervising agent before derivation begins; the derivation producer must not choose it. This separation of selection authority from scoring machinery is what makes adversarial context selection a detectable event rather than a policy choice.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the authorized task context is an input, never an output kappa_star is selected by the task controller / scheduler / supervising agent before derivation begins; the derivation producer must not choose it. this separation of selection authority from scoring machinery is what makes adversarial context selection a detectable event rather than a policy choice. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Class names are context-independent; interpretations are not

framing/ontology

A source label stores a context-independent class name, but the order and interval that interpret it are context-specific. A record written under one context is not automatically authorized under another merely because the class name is shared — reuse requires an explicit tau_{kappa'} valuation or an audited revaluation step.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
class names are context-independent; interpretations are not a source label stores a context-independent class name, but the order and interval that interpret it are context-specific. a record written under one context is not automatically authorized under another merely because the class name is shared — reuse requires an explicit tau_{kappa'} valuation or an audited revaluation step. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Partial valuation with no fallback filling

mechanism

tau: Records × Contexts → [0,1] is deliberately partial. Missing context valuations are never defaulted: a consumer wanting a record in an unvalued context must evaluate, translate, quarantine, or reject it.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
partial valuation with no fallback filling tau: records × contexts → [0,1] is deliberately partial. missing context valuations are never defaulted: a consumer wanting a record in an unvalued context must evaluate, translate, quarantine, or reject it. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Three concrete context policies (fixture)

experiment/benchmark

Perception (fab≺sim≺infer≺react≺tool≺ext), simulation planner (fab≺react≺infer≺sim≺tool≺ext — sim promoted above react and infer), forensic audit (perception order but stricter intervals, e.g. fab capped at 0.10). Explicit interval tables so conformance failures attribute to concrete policy, not hidden interpretation.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
three concrete context policies (fixture) perception (fab≺sim≺infer≺react≺tool≺ext), simulation planner (fab≺react≺infer≺sim≺tool≺ext — sim promoted above react and infer), forensic audit (perception order but stricter intervals, e.g. fab capped at 0.10). explicit interval tables so conformance failures attribute to concrete policy, not hidden interpretation. experiment/benchmark papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Contextual integrity meet + class-selection function with tie convention

mechanism

The permitted label is the least-integrity class among the operation ceiling and contributors under the active context's order; the class-selection function then returns the maximum class at-or-below the permitted meet whose interval contains the capped scalar, rejecting if none exists. Closed intervals mean a boundary scalar can belong to two adjacent classes; the deterministic tie rule is the maximum eligible class, and no tie can exceed the meet.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
contextual integrity meet + class-selection function with tie convention the permitted label is the least-integrity class among the operation ceiling and contributors under the active context's order; the class-selection function then returns the maximum class at-or-below the permitted meet whose interval contains the capped scalar, rejecting if none exists. closed intervals mean a boundary scalar can belong to two adjacent classes; the deterministic tie rule is the maximum eligible class, and no tie can exceed the meet. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Conservative class lowering, never raising

mechanism

When the capped scalar falls strictly below the permitted class interval, the label is conservatively lowered to a class whose interval contains it (or the write is quarantined/rejected) — the scalar and class may never be raised to satisfy the interval. The selected class is "an integrity-admissibility label, not a free-form narrative of origin."

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
conservative class lowering, never raising when the capped scalar falls strictly below the permitted class interval, the label is conservatively lowered to a class whose interval contains it (or the write is quarantined/rejected) — the scalar and class may never be raised to satisfy the interval. the selected class is an integrity-admissibility label, not a free-form narrative of origin. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Separate routing class from literal production channel in production schemas

mechanism

Because class-selection can lower the context-integrity class below the literal production channel, a production schema that needs literal channel names should store the original production source separately from the selected context-integrity class (routing/admissibility field vs explanation field), or refine low-integrity outcomes into explicit quarantine subclasses. The artifact reuses the Source field only for API compatibility.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
separate routing class from literal production channel in production schemas because class-selection can lower the context-integrity class below the literal production channel, a production schema that needs literal channel names should store the original production source separately from the selected context-integrity class (routing/admissibility field vs explanation field), or refine low-integrity outcomes into explicit quarantine subclasses. the artifact reuses the source field only for api compatibility. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Context-capped inscription: five preconditions

mechanism

Before a write: the active context exists and is content-addressable; every contributor has a defined tau under kappa_star; every contributor is context-class-consistent; the operation has a defined ceiling; the witness records ordered direct contributors and the exact transitive closure. Contributors imported from other contexts must be revalued, translated through an audited operation, quarantined, or rejected first. Precondition failures raise errors — never silently defaulted.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
context-capped inscription: five preconditions before a write: the active context exists and is content-addressable; every contributor has a defined tau under kappa_star; every contributor is context-class-consistent; the operation has a defined ceiling; the witness records ordered direct contributors and the exact transitive closure. contributors imported from other contexts must be revalued, translated through an audited operation, quarantined, or rejected first. precondition failures raise errors — never silently defaulted. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The policy hash commits the whole policy object

mechanism

The recorded hash covers a canonical serialization of schema version, context name, source order, per-class interval bounds, and the operation-to-ceiling mapping. Any change produces a new hash, making silent policy substitution detectable by hash recomputation.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the policy hash commits the whole policy object the recorded hash covers a canonical serialization of schema version, context name, source order, per-class interval bounds, and the operation-to-ceiling mapping. any change produces a new hash, making silent policy substitution detectable by hash recomputation. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Safe-score construction: cap first, then class-select

mechanism

Given an unconstrained quality-model proposal q(r): compute m = min(q, operation ceiling, permitted-class upper endpoint, contributor scalars); set tau = m; class-select for m; reject if no class. Order matters: interval correction must never increase the scalar — upward clipping after the min amplifies, downward clipping before the min short-circuits the cap. m is the canonical maximum informative score for a committed proposal; audit can verify admissibility but reconstructs the producer's exact scalar only if the proposal (or its deterministic rule) is committed.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
safe-score construction: cap first, then class-select given an unconstrained quality-model proposal q(r): compute m = min(q, operation ceiling, permitted-class upper endpoint, contributor scalars); set tau = m; class-select for m; reject if no class. order matters: interval correction must never increase the scalar — upward clipping after the min amplifies, downward clipping before the min short-circuits the cap. m is the canonical maximum informative score for a committed proposal; audit can verify admissibility but reconstructs the producer's exact scalar only if the proposal (or its deterministic rule) is committed. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Safe-score lemma with degenerate-input handling

result/finding

The construction terminates with a non-empty selected class whenever m ∈ [0,1]; q(r) < 0 forces rejection (out of every interval); q(r) > 1 is harmless (other cap terms dominate). The non-empty-selection argument needs only the meet direction, not its converse.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
safe-score lemma with degenerate-input handling the construction terminates with a non-empty selected class whenever m ∈ [0,1]; q(r) < 0 forces rejection (out of every interval); q(r) > 1 is harmless (other cap terms dominate). the non-empty-selection argument needs only the meet direction, not its converse. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Interval-boundary variant (half-open intervals)

mechanism

Deployments preferring stricter lowering can use half-open intervals [l,u) except at the top class, without altering the cap — a named deterministic implementation variant kept out of the main theorem.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
interval-boundary variant (half-open intervals) deployments preferring stricter lowering can use half-open intervals [l,u) except at the top class, without altering the cap — a named deterministic implementation variant kept out of the main theorem. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Three-way audit decomposition: local replay / authorization / policy verification

mechanism

Local replay recomputes label, cap, class selection, and provenance under the recorded context (internal consistency); authorization compares kappa_rec against the independently logged kappa_star; policy verification resolves the recorded hash to an immutable policy object. Intentionally separable: a record can pass replay while failing authorization (wrong context) or policy verification (silent substitution), and the audit must report which check failed.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
three-way audit decomposition: local replay / authorization / policy verification local replay recomputes label, cap, class selection, and provenance under the recorded context (internal consistency); authorization compares kappa_rec against the independently logged kappa_star; policy verification resolves the recorded hash to an immutable policy object. intentionally separable: a record can pass replay while failing authorization (wrong context) or policy verification (silent substitution), and the audit must report which check failed. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The hash is authoritative; the stored order is documentation

mechanism

Inscription metadata carries both a human-readable source order and a policy hash. At replay, the authoritative policy is the one resolved from the hash; if the resolved policy disagrees with the stored copy, the recorded order is treated as a tampered annotation, not a competing source of truth.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the hash is authoritative; the stored order is documentation inscription metadata carries both a human-readable source order and a policy hash. at replay, the authoritative policy is the one resolved from the hash; if the resolved policy disagrees with the stored copy, the recorded order is treated as a tampered annotation, not a competing source of truth. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Local-replay observational equivalence lemma

result/finding

Any two policy states that induce the same permitted source, cap, class selection, and closure on a fixed witness are indistinguishable to local replay — distinguishing them requires the authorization log or the content-addressed policy hash. This is the formal statement of why replay alone cannot catch adversarial context selection or silent policy substitution.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
local-replay observational equivalence lemma any two policy states that induce the same permitted source, cap, class selection, and closure on a fixed witness are indistinguishable to local replay — distinguishing them requires the authorization log or the content-addressed policy hash. this is the formal statement of why replay alone cannot catch adversarial context selection or silent policy substitution. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Degraded mode without an authorization log

mechanism

If the independent AuthLog is unavailable, the auditor can still run local replay under the recorded context but cannot detect adversarial selection or policy substitution: the triple equality degrades gracefully to the single check kappa_score = kappa_rec. The paper explicitly refuses to claim more in that setting.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
degraded mode without an authorization log if the independent authlog is unavailable, the auditor can still run local replay under the recorded context but cannot detect adversarial selection or policy substitution: the triple equality degrades gracefully to the single check kappa_score = kappa_rec. the paper explicitly refuses to claim more in that setting. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ AuthLog and PolicyRegistry as typed TCB objects

mechanism

AuthLog: EventID ⇀ (kappa_star, h_star), an append-only map written by the task controller before writeback, binding each derivation event to its authorized context and policy hash. PolicyRegistry: Hash ⇀ policy object, immutable and injective (content-addressed storage). The audit theorem quantifies exactly what these two artifacts buy when paired with local replay.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
authlog and policyregistry as typed tcb objects authlog: eventid ⇀ (kappa_star, h_star), an append-only map written by the task controller before writeback, binding each derivation event to its authorized context and policy hash. policyregistry: hash ⇀ policy object, immutable and injective (content-addressed storage). the audit theorem quantifies exactly what these two artifacts buy when paired with local replay. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ TCB-relative admissible audit soundness theorem

result/finding

Against an adversary controlling the derivation producer (content, proposed score, label, recorded context, hash, malformed witnesses) but not the TCB (task controller, AuthLog, PolicyRegistry, canonical serialization, hash function, contributor store, replay checker), any accepted inscription satisfies admissible fixed-context non-amplification under the task-authorized context and policy version. Explicitly does NOT claim the stored scalar equals the canonical safe score unless the proposal score or deterministic rule is also committed — admissibility, not equality.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
tcb-relative admissible audit soundness theorem against an adversary controlling the derivation producer (content, proposed score, label, recorded context, hash, malformed witnesses) but not the tcb (task controller, authlog, policyregistry, canonical serialization, hash function, contributor store, replay checker), any accepted inscription satisfies admissible fixed-context non-amplification under the task-authorized context and policy version. explicitly does not claim the stored scalar equals the canonical safe score unless the proposal score or deterministic rule is also committed — admissibility, not equality. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Failure attribution: four checks ↔ four named metrics

mechanism

Witness availability failures → PRF (provenance-witness failure); local replay failures → RMR; policy-hash failures → PVF; authorization failures → CAMR. A failed audit points to which trusted-boundary check rejected the attempt.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
failure attribution: four checks ↔ four named metrics witness availability failures → prf (provenance-witness failure); local replay failures → rmr; policy-hash failures → pvf; authorization failures → camr. a failed audit points to which trusted-boundary check rejected the attempt. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Cross-context derivation paths need audited translation edges

mechanism

Path non-amplification holds only over same-context sub-DAGs. A record valued under kappa_1 does not automatically get a tau_{kappa_2}; every context transition must itself be an accepted, ceiling-bounded translation operation before a single-context scalar comparison is meaningful.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
cross-context derivation paths need audited translation edges path non-amplification holds only over same-context sub-dags. a record valued under kappa_1 does not automatically get a tau_{kappa_2}; every context transition must itself be an accepted, ceiling-bounded translation operation before a single-context scalar comparison is meaningful. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Replay detects observable mismatches, not counterfactual ones

result/finding

If the scoring and recorded contexts happen to induce the same cap and selected class for the stored scalar and witness, replay may pass and has no observable evidence of context inflation for that write. The proposition is honest about detecting observable mismatches only — the residue is exactly what authorization and policy hashing exist for.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
replay detects observable mismatches, not counterfactual ones if the scoring and recorded contexts happen to induce the same cap and selected class for the stored scalar and witness, replay may pass and has no observable evidence of context inflation for that write. the proposition is honest about detecting observable mismatches only — the residue is exactly what authorization and policy hashing exist for. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Static valuation as a degenerate special case

result/finding

With one context whose order and intervals equal the per-record policy, context-capped inscription reduces exactly to per-record scalar valuation — the collapse corollary establishing strict generalization.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
static valuation as a degenerate special case with one context whose order and intervals equal the per-record policy, context-capped inscription reduces exactly to per-record scalar valuation — the collapse corollary establishing strict generalization. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The five negative controls + three hash-carrying variants

experiment/benchmark

Static per-record (perception order as global), implicit context (correct values, context not inscribed), context mismatch (score under planner, record the step context), adversarial context selection (pick the score-maximizing context, record it honestly), and context-aware-no-policy-hash. The +hash variants of static/mismatch/adversarial test whether a valid policy hash repairs other failures.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the five negative controls + three hash-carrying variants static per-record (perception order as global), implicit context (correct values, context not inscribed), context mismatch (score under planner, record the step context), adversarial context selection (pick the score-maximizing context, record it honestly), and context-aware-no-policy-hash. the +hash variants of static/mismatch/adversarial test whether a valid policy hash repairs other failures. experiment/benchmark papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Hashes are not a universal repair

result/finding

Adding a valid policy hash drops PVF from 1.00 to 0.00 for the three augmented controls but leaves every core failure unchanged: "Hashes verify policy provenance; they do not make a static valuation context-sensitive, make a planner-scored value replay under perception, or make an adversarially chosen permissive context task-authorized." The results tables are deliberately split so hash availability cannot make the proposed rule win by construction.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
hashes are not a universal repair adding a valid policy hash drops pvf from 1.00 to 0.00 for the three augmented controls but leaves every core failure unchanged: hashes verify policy provenance; they do not make a static valuation context-sensitive, make a planner-scored value replay under perception, or make an adversarially chosen permissive context task-authorized. the results tables are deliberately split so hash availability cannot make the proposed rule win by construction. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Adversarial context selection: the honest-but-permissive attacker

mechanism

The attacker picks the context maximizing the scalar bound and inscribes it honestly — replay passes (internally consistent), missing-context is zero, and the only trace is authorization mismatch (CAMR 0.27). Together with context mismatch it brackets the two ways to break the triple equality: lie about which context authorized a value (caught by replay), or pick a permissive context honestly (caught only by the authorization log).

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
adversarial context selection: the honest-but-permissive attacker the attacker picks the context maximizing the scalar bound and inscribes it honestly — replay passes (internally consistent), missing-context is zero, and the only trace is authorization mismatch (camr 0.27). together with context mismatch it brackets the two ways to break the triple equality: lie about which context authorized a value (caught by replay), or pick a permissive context honestly (caught only by the authorization log). mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Implicit context: numerically safe, structurally unauditable

result/finding

The implicit-context control computes correct context-specific values but doesn't inscribe the context: zero writeback violations, yet missing-context rate 1.00 and replay-mismatch 1.00 because there is nothing to replay against. Correctness of values is not auditability of values.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
implicit context: numerically safe, structurally unauditable the implicit-context control computes correct context-specific values but doesn't inscribe the context: zero writeback violations, yet missing-context rate 1.00 and replay-mismatch 1.00 because there is nothing to replay against. correctness of values is not auditability of values. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The minimal-pair control: context-aware without policy hash

result/finding

A control identical to the proposed rule in every behavior except omitting the policy hash: all core metrics clean, PVF = 1.00. This minimal pair demonstrates the policy-hash field "is not a redundant decoration --- it is the difference between an inscription whose origin can be replayed and an inscription whose policy provenance is silent."

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the minimal-pair control: context-aware without policy hash a control identical to the proposed rule in every behavior except omitting the policy hash: all core metrics clean, pvf = 1.00. this minimal pair demonstrates the policy-hash field is not a redundant decoration --- it is the difference between an inscription whose origin can be replayed and an inscription whose policy provenance is silent. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Nine coined conformance metrics

coinage

CCVR (context-ceiling violation rate), CIVR (context-interval violation rate), FHTR (false high-trust routing), MCR (missing-context rate), CAMR (context-authorization mismatch rate), PVF (policy-verification failure), RMR (replay-mismatch rate), LDM (label-decision mismatch), CSS (context-sensitivity success), plus PRF (provenance-witness failure). MCR and CAMR are mutually exclusive by construction, covering the two ways context can be wrong: missing or misauthorized.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
nine coined conformance metrics ccvr (context-ceiling violation rate), civr (context-interval violation rate), fhtr (false high-trust routing), mcr (missing-context rate), camr (context-authorization mismatch rate), pvf (policy-verification failure), rmr (replay-mismatch rate), ldm (label-decision mismatch), css (context-sensitivity success), plus prf (provenance-witness failure). mcr and camr are mutually exclusive by construction, covering the two ways context can be wrong: missing or misauthorized. coinage papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ FHTR: a precise definition of "contaminated"

coinage

A derived record is contaminated when its permitted source ranks strictly below infer under the task-authorized context; it routes high when its stored label ranks at/above infer or its stored scalar strictly exceeds the infer interval floor. FHTR is measured over the contaminated subset only (denominator 3 of 11 in the fixture).

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
fhtr: a precise definition of contaminated a derived record is contaminated when its permitted source ranks strictly below infer under the task-authorized context; it routes high when its stored label ranks at/above infer or its stored scalar strictly exceeds the infer interval floor. fhtr is measured over the contaminated subset only (denominator 3 of 11 in the fixture). coinage papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The recomputable fixture: 4 base records, 6 case families, 11 derived writes

experiment/benchmark

Base records E_SENSOR (ext; 0.98/0.94/0.96 across contexts), S_MODEL (sim; 0.22/0.72/0.20), T_TOOL (tool; 0.84/0.86/0.82), F_NOTE (fab; 0.08/0.07/0.05). Case families: perception contamination, planner simulated projection, same-inputs context contrast, planner long chain, five-step planner deep chain (alternating sim and derivation ops, final step re-mixes a derived record with the original external seed), forensic fabrication contamination. Every expected label and cap is published so an independent reader can recompute every metric.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the recomputable fixture: 4 base records, 6 case families, 11 derived writes base records e_sensor (ext; 0.98/0.94/0.96 across contexts), s_model (sim; 0.22/0.72/0.20), t_tool (tool; 0.84/0.86/0.82), f_note (fab; 0.08/0.07/0.05). case families: perception contamination, planner simulated projection, same-inputs context contrast, planner long chain, five-step planner deep chain (alternating sim and derivation ops, final step re-mixes a derived record with the original external seed), forensic fabrication contamination. every expected label and cap is published so an independent reader can recompute every metric. experiment/benchmark papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Deterministic control numbers (CDV)

result/finding

context_capped: all zero, CSS 1.00. static: CCVR 0.09, CIVR 0.73, CAMR 0.82, LDM 0.36, CSS 0.00 (always records perception, so replay passes; the failure is the recorded context is never the task-authorized one for non-perception steps). context_mismatch: CCVR 0.27, CIVR 0.09, FHTR 0.67, RMR 0.27, CSS 0.50. adversarial: same core numbers but CAMR 0.27 and RMR 0.00. All rates decompose into exact small-denominator fractions (0.27=3/11, 0.67=2/3, 0.82=9/11 …) with counts in the JSON.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
deterministic control numbers (cdv) context_capped: all zero, css 1.00. static: ccvr 0.09, civr 0.73, camr 0.82, ldm 0.36, css 0.00 (always records perception, so replay passes; the failure is the recorded context is never the task-authorized one for non-perception steps). context_mismatch: ccvr 0.27, civr 0.09, fhtr 0.67, rmr 0.27, css 0.50. adversarial: same core numbers but camr 0.27 and rmr 0.00. all rates decompose into exact small-denominator fractions (0.27=3/11, 0.67=2/3, 0.82=9/11 …) with counts in the json. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Jitter ladder: robustness one class-interval wide

result/finding

The 20-seed sweep is repeated at jitter scales {0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.10}; at 0.10 individual contributor scores can be displaced by roughly one class-interval width. The proposed rule stays at zero everywhere; the adversarial baseline keeps its exact attack signature across the ladder, showing the failure is induced by context selection, not one fixture value.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
jitter ladder: robustness one class-interval wide the 20-seed sweep is repeated at jitter scales {0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.10}; at 0.10 individual contributor scores can be displaced by roughly one class-interval width. the proposed rule stays at zero everywhere; the adversarial baseline keeps its exact attack signature across the ladder, showing the failure is induced by context selection, not one fixture value. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Property-based conformance: 3,047 derivations, zero counterexamples

result/finding

500 trials generate random total source orders, random non-crossing interval policies covering [0,1], random class-consistent base records, and random derivation DAGs; seven properties checked per derivation (scalar non-amplification, class consistency, permitted label, authorization, policy hash, replay, exact witness). 3,047 completed, 1,953 skipped at the precondition layer because a contributor lacked tau for the chosen context — and the ~2-in-5 skip rate is itself presented as informative: precondition rejection is the prescribed behavior. Failing seeds would be written to property_fail_seeds for diagnostic replay.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
property-based conformance: 3,047 derivations, zero counterexamples 500 trials generate random total source orders, random non-crossing interval policies covering [0,1], random class-consistent base records, and random derivation dags; seven properties checked per derivation (scalar non-amplification, class consistency, permitted label, authorization, policy hash, replay, exact witness). 3,047 completed, 1,953 skipped at the precondition layer because a contributor lacked tau for the chosen context — and the ~2-in-5 skip rate is itself presented as informative: precondition rejection is the prescribed behavior. failing seeds would be written to `property_fail_seeds` for diagnostic replay. result/finding papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Policy-substitution mutation: same name, same cap, different policy

experiment/benchmark

A targeted test derives a record under policy version A and replays against version B that keeps the same context name and the same relevant cap on the witness — local replay passes by observational equivalence while hash verification rejects. This exercises PVF as more than a missing-field check and is impossible for the no-policy-hash baseline.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
policy-substitution mutation: same name, same cap, different policy a targeted test derives a record under policy version a and replays against version b that keeps the same context name and the same relevant cap on the witness — local replay passes by observational equivalence while hash verification rejects. this exercises pvf as more than a missing-field check and is impossible for the no-policy-hash baseline. experiment/benchmark papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Context is part of the derivation witness, analogous to provenance

framing/ontology

The key design decision framed as ontology: a context is not merely an argument to a downstream router; it is part of the derivation witness. "This makes the valuation context analogous to provenance: not a decoration, but part of the integrity evidence."

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
context is part of the derivation witness, analogous to provenance the key design decision framed as ontology: a context is not merely an argument to a downstream router; it is part of the derivation witness. this makes the valuation context analogous to provenance: not a decoration, but part of the integrity evidence. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The safe artifact tuple

mechanism

The complete unit that must travel with a record for it to remain auditable: (Source(r), tau_kappa(r), kappa, h(kappa), o, Direct(r), Prov(r)). A scalar without its context is ambiguous; a label without its context ordering is incomplete; context metadata without contributors cannot replay.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the safe artifact tuple the complete unit that must travel with a record for it to remain auditable: (source(r), tau_kappa(r), kappa, h(kappa), o, direct(r), prov(r)). a scalar without its context is ambiguous; a label without its context ordering is incomplete; context metadata without contributors cannot replay. mechanism papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ The "third question" positioning against IFC/declassification/contextual integrity/provenance

framing/ontology

Prior frameworks answer what a value's origin is and where it may flow. CDV pins a third question: when the same source class can legitimately have different scalar integrity interpretations under different task contexts, and when the scoring context itself must be bound into the inscription witness. Vs declassification: conservative rather than relaxing. Vs Nissenbaum's contextual integrity: the accounting unit is a writeback step, not an information flow between actors. Vs database provenance: the contribution is not the closure relation but that the deriving policy be content-addressable and replayable.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
the third question positioning against ifc/declassification/contextual integrity/provenance prior frameworks answer what a value's origin is and where it may flow. cdv pins a third question: when the same source class can legitimately have different scalar integrity interpretations under different task contexts, and when the scoring context itself must be bound into the inscription witness. vs declassification: conservative rather than relaxing. vs nissenbaum's contextual integrity: the accounting unit is a writeback step, not an information flow between actors. vs database provenance: the contribution is not the closure relation but that the deriving policy be content-addressable and replayable. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ CDV threat model / non-defenses

framing/ontology

Defends against context laundering at writeback (permissive scoring context, omitted metadata, mismatched recording). Does not defend against compromised source classification, missing contributors, poisoned retrieval without writeback, adversarial calibration of base tau values, or cross-agent serialization stripping metadata — the last named explicitly as requiring transport-layer preservation of the safe tuple or treating imports as unaudited.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
cdv threat model / non-defenses defends against context laundering at writeback (permissive scoring context, omitted metadata, mismatched recording). does not defend against compromised source classification, missing contributors, poisoned retrieval without writeback, adversarial calibration of base tau values, or cross-agent serialization stripping metadata — the last named explicitly as requiring transport-layer preservation of the safe tuple or treating imports as unaudited. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Biological analogy with a declared stopping point

metaphor

Human source monitoring as design intuition: a dream or imagined future is not deleted; it stays usable only when later cognition respects the source tag attached at encoding — kappa plays the engineering role of making that tag task-relative and auditable. The paper then declares where the analogy stops: no claim that hippocampal indexing or reality monitoring implement tau_kappa caps.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
biological analogy with a declared stopping point human source monitoring as design intuition: a dream or imagined future is not deleted; it stays usable only when later cognition respects the source tag attached at encoding — kappa plays the engineering role of making that tag task-relative and auditable. the paper then declares where the analogy stops: no claim that hippocampal indexing or reality monitoring implement tau_kappa caps. metaphor papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ CDV limitations as the invariant's boundary

open-question

(1) Base tau values are fixture-assigned, not learned; (2) context policies are hand-specified — production needs policy design/governance/validation procedures; (3) complete contributor sets assumed; (4) no adversarial source classifiers, retrieval poisoning, or signed transport tested; (5) total orders and non-crossing intervals only — incomparable classes or overlapping evidential bands are outside the theorem unless reduced to a total policy before inscription; (6) contributor records must remain available at replay. Framed as "the boundary of the present invariant rather than a separate research agenda."

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
cdv limitations as the invariant's boundary (1) base tau values are fixture-assigned, not learned; (2) context policies are hand-specified — production needs policy design/governance/validation procedures; (3) complete contributor sets assumed; (4) no adversarial source classifiers, retrieval poisoning, or signed transport tested; (5) total orders and non-crossing intervals only — incomparable classes or overlapping evidential bands are outside the theorem unless reduced to a total policy before inscription; (6) contributor records must remain available at replay. framed as the boundary of the present invariant rather than a separate research agenda. open-question papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex

◈ Chain-order discipline (roadmap position paragraph)

framing/ontology

The paper explicitly places itself downstream of per-record valuation and the standalone credibility layer, follows the stated order of work, and refuses to use credibility results in the fixed-context theorem — the citation-order discipline that keeps each paper's assumed guarantees explicit.

papers\Context_Dependent_Valuation.tex
---
chain-order discipline (roadmap position paragraph) the paper explicitly places itself downstream of per-record valuation and the standalone credibility layer, follows the stated order of work, and refuses to use credibility results in the fixed-context theorem — the citation-order discipline that keeps each paper's assumed guarantees explicit. framing/ontology papers\context_dependent_valuation.tex ---

Label Transport (LT)

◈ Signed provenance envelopes (the core mechanism)

mechanism

Every cross-boundary message is a content-bound envelope carrying the full inscription witness produced by the chain so far — source label, scalar, recorded context + policy hash, credibility-log head + registry anchor, operation, ordered contributor witness, transitive provenance closure, issuing taxonomy, issuer policy bundle, issuer identity, timestamp — under one signature over the canonical serialization of all of them.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
signed provenance envelopes (the core mechanism) every cross-boundary message is a content-bound envelope carrying the full inscription witness produced by the chain so far — source label, scalar, recorded context + policy hash, credibility-log head + registry anchor, operation, ordered contributor witness, transitive provenance closure, issuing taxonomy, issuer policy bundle, issuer identity, timestamp — under one signature over the canonical serialization of all of them. mechanism papers\label_transport_draft.tex

◈ The headline boundary law

framing/ontology

The compact statement of the whole paper, boxed in the abstract.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
"transport may translate labels, but it must not promote them; the meet at the trust boundary is the meet of the foundation, evaluated at import."
the headline boundary law the compact statement of the whole paper, boxed in the abstract. framing/ontology papers\label_transport_draft.tex transport may translate labels, but it must not promote them; the meet at the trust boundary is the meet of the foundation, evaluated at import.

◈ The transport gap: every prior guarantee is conditional on correct labels at the boundary

framing/ontology

Source downgrading, per-record valuation, time-varying credibility, and context-dependent valuation each prove invariants assuming inputs carry the labels/scalars/logs/contexts the prior layers require. At agent/service/persistence boundaries that assumption is easiest to violate: labels can be stripped, upgraded, re-routed, or re-interpreted in transit, after which every downstream meet silently operates on laundered inputs.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
the transport gap: every prior guarantee is conditional on correct labels at the boundary source downgrading, per-record valuation, time-varying credibility, and context-dependent valuation each prove invariants assuming inputs carry the labels/scalars/logs/contexts the prior layers require. at agent/service/persistence boundaries that assumption is easiest to violate: labels can be stripped, upgraded, re-routed, or re-interpreted in transit, after which every downstream meet silently operates on laundered inputs. framing/ontology papers\label_transport_draft.tex

◈ Import as the inverse of inscription

framing/ontology

Inscription assumes labeled inputs and bounds the output. Import receives unlabeled, ambiguously labeled, or maliciously labeled inputs and must decide what to bind them to before any local meet applies. Structurally, import is the only inscription event in the chain whose contributors must be verified before the meet can be soundly applied — the contributor list is by construction drawn from outside the local labeling discipline.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
import as the inverse of inscription inscription assumes labeled inputs and bounds the output. import receives unlabeled, ambiguously labeled, or maliciously labeled inputs and must decide what to bind them to before any local meet applies. structurally, import is the only inscription event in the chain whose contributors must be verified before the meet can be soundly applied — the contributor list is by construction drawn from outside the local labeling discipline. framing/ontology papers\label_transport_draft.tex

◈ Three structural differences of import vs local inscription

framing/ontology

(1) Adversarial inputs — foreign contributors can't be re-resolved through the local index; the envelope, not a lookup, is the witness. (2) Foreign taxonomy — string-equality class matching is unsound (same string may name incomparable classes, different strings equivalent ones). (3) Foreign policy — interval policies, kernel roots, and context hashes refer to registries the importer may not host, requiring cross-registry attestation or downgrade.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
three structural differences of import vs local inscription (1) adversarial inputs — foreign contributors can't be re-resolved through the local index; the envelope, not a lookup, is the witness. (2) foreign taxonomy — string-equality class matching is unsound (same string may name incomparable classes, different strings equivalent ones). (3) foreign policy — interval policies, kernel roots, and context hashes refer to registries the importer may not host, requiring cross-registry attestation or downgrade. framing/ontology papers\label_transport_draft.tex

◈ Why label transport closes the chain

framing/ontology

The prior extensions are independently deployable; label transport composes across them because the boundary carries every artifact they produce — the class+closure (foundation), scalar+interval policy (per-record), credibility head+anchor (TVC), and context+policy hash (CDV). Without one envelope tying all four under one signature, the boundary is the weakest point of the entire chain.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
why label transport closes the chain the prior extensions are independently deployable; label transport composes across them because the boundary carries every artifact they produce — the class+closure (foundation), scalar+interval policy (per-record), credibility head+anchor (tvc), and context+policy hash (cdv). without one envelope tying all four under one signature, the boundary is the weakest point of the entire chain. framing/ontology papers\label_transport_draft.tex

◈ Three transit failure modes handled by distinct envelope components

framing/ontology

Label stripping (channel drops the label; safe default rejects/quarantines as source-uncertain; naive default treats as tool output and restores laundering), label promotion in transit (MITM raises sim to ext; rejected by content-label cryptographic binding), taxonomy substitution ("sim at A equals react at B" silently amplifies; blocked by a registered, policy-relative non-amplifying translation).

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
three transit failure modes handled by distinct envelope components label stripping (channel drops the label; safe default rejects/quarantines as source-uncertain; naive default treats as tool output and restores laundering), label promotion in transit (mitm raises sim to ext; rejected by content-label cryptographic binding), taxonomy substitution ( sim at a equals react at b silently amplifies; blocked by a registered, policy-relative non-amplifying translation). framing/ontology papers\label_transport_draft.tex

◈ Prior chain as a five-part contract on inscription inputs

framing/ontology

A self-contained restatement so readers can check LT's results without the prior papers: (1) total source order with meet+operation-ceiling; (2) per-class scalar interval policy; (3) append-only credibility log folded by a registered kernel; (4) authorized inscription context with policy hash; (5) the composite inscription witness W(r) that audit replay re-verifies. LT treats these as preconditions on the foreign witness, not theorems to re-prove.

papers\Label_Transport_draft.tex
