self-vs-truth laundering audit and cascade invisibility the trace-memory audit returns paired self-vs-truth metrics designed to detect cascade-invisibility — a self-only audit explicitly warns that truth labels are absent and cannot declare clean, acknowledging that a system auditing itself with its own labels can be blind to cascaded laundering. mechanism docs/trace-memory-library.md ---

Per-Record Trust Valuation (paper)

◈ Per-record trust valuation: scalar trust inside class intervals

mechanism

Extend source downgrading from one rank per class to a per-record scalar tau(r) ∈ [0,1], with each class assigned an interval [tau_c^-, tau_c^+] that must be order-consistent (a ≺ b ⟹ tau_a^+ ≤ tau_b^-). Two external sensors of different calibration, or a signed API vs an unaudited scraper, can now be distinguished within a class — without disturbing the lattice. Collapses exactly to static source downgrading when intervals degenerate to points.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex, docs/ROADMAP.md
"per-record trust may vary within a class, but derived trust may not amplify beyond support."
per-record trust valuation: scalar trust inside class intervals extend source downgrading from one rank per class to a per-record scalar `tau(r) ∈ [0,1]`, with each class assigned an interval `[tau_c^-, tau_c^+]` that must be order-consistent (`a ≺ b ⟹ tau_a^+ ≤ tau_b^-`). two external sensors of different calibration, or a signed api vs an unaudited scraper, can now be distinguished within a class — without disturbing the lattice. collapses exactly to static source downgrading when intervals degenerate to points. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex, docs/roadmap.md per-record trust may vary within a class, but derived trust may not amplify beyond support.

◈ Confidence laundering (coinage)

coinage

The scalar analogue of inference laundering: if a derived record's score is computed by averaging, voting, self-rated confidence, or downstream confirmation without a contributor-minimum cap, many high-trust contributors can mask a materially necessary low-trust contaminant. "This is not merely poor calibration."

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
confidence laundering (coinage) the scalar analogue of inference laundering: if a derived record's score is computed by averaging, voting, self-rated confidence, or downstream confirmation without a contributor-minimum cap, many high-trust contributors can mask a materially necessary low-trust contaminant. this is not merely poor calibration. coinage papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ The averaging trap (worked failure)

result/finding

Ten external records at 0.95 plus one materially-necessary simulated record at 0.30: averaging yields ~0.89–0.905, near external range; the naive policy even promotes the class to ext. The capped rule instead writes class sim, tau 0.30. This single example is instantiated as a fixture case and drives the headline comparison.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
the averaging trap (worked failure) ten external records at 0.95 plus one materially-necessary simulated record at 0.30: averaging yields ~0.89–0.905, near external range; the naive policy even promotes the class to `ext`. the capped rule instead writes class `sim`, tau 0.30. this single example is instantiated as a fixture case and drives the headline comparison. result/finding papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Valuation-capped inscription (five-step rule)

mechanism

Derived write algorithm: (1) candidate class by the class meet; (2) scalar cap B = min(beta_tau(o), tau^+_class, tau(c_1..c_n)); (3) provenance closure; (4) candidate score min(q(r), B) — upward clipping never permitted; (5) if the capped score falls below the candidate interval, the write must lower the class, quarantine, or reject — never raise the scalar to fit. The class-lowering branch replaces earlier bidirectional clipping schemes with a strictly one-sided cap.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
valuation-capped inscription (five-step rule) derived write algorithm: (1) candidate class by the class meet; (2) scalar cap `b = min(beta_tau(o), tau^+_class, tau(c_1..c_n))`; (3) provenance closure; (4) candidate score `min(q(r), b)` — upward clipping never permitted; (5) if the capped score falls below the candidate interval, the write must lower the class, quarantine, or reject — never raise the scalar to fit. the class-lowering branch replaces earlier bidirectional clipping schemes with a strictly one-sided cap. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Class is never derived from the scalar (touching-boundary rule)

mechanism

Interval membership is a consistency check on the meet-assigned class, not a class-derivation from tau. At a touching boundary value tau_a^+ = tau_b^- shared by two intervals, the active class is whichever the meet assigned — never the higher class that also numerically contains the value. Prevents scalar-routing ambiguity from becoming promotion.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
class is never derived from the scalar (touching-boundary rule) interval membership is a consistency check on the meet-assigned class, not a class-derivation from tau. at a touching boundary value `tau_a^+ = tau_b^-` shared by two intervals, the active class is whichever the meet assigned — never the higher class that also numerically contains the value. prevents scalar-routing ambiguity from becoming promotion. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Material contributor definition

mechanism

A contributor is material if removing it from the derivation witness would change the asserted content or invalidate the derivation step. The min-cap is sound only over material contributors; retrieved-but-unused and redundant alternate-proof inputs may be excluded. Detecting materiality at runtime is explicitly out of scope (needs sealed contexts, derivation witnesses, or support-graph tracers).

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
material contributor definition a contributor is material if removing it from the derivation witness would change the asserted content or invalidate the derivation step. the min-cap is sound only over material contributors; retrieved-but-unused and redundant alternate-proof inputs may be excluded. detecting materiality at runtime is explicitly out of scope (needs sealed contexts, derivation witnesses, or support-graph tracers). mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Support certificates and inscription-bound certificate selection

mechanism

When multiple sufficient contributor sets exist for the same derived content (disjunctive support), each certificate induces its own capped valuation, and routing may choose among them — but the selected certificate must be bound to the inscription event by a witness identifier and cannot be substituted ex post to obtain a higher valuation: "replacing the inscription-time certificate with an alternative high-trust certificate after the fact is a laundering operation, not a re-valuation."

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
support certificates and inscription-bound certificate selection when multiple sufficient contributor sets exist for the same derived content (disjunctive support), each certificate induces its own capped valuation, and routing may choose among them — but the selected certificate must be bound to the inscription event by a witness identifier and cannot be substituted ex post to obtain a higher valuation: replacing the inscription-time certificate with an alternative high-trust certificate after the fact is a laundering operation, not a re-valuation. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Scalar operation ceilings

mechanism

beta_tau(derived)=tau^+_infer, beta_tau(simulated)=tau^+_sim, beta_tau(fabricated)=tau^+_fab — even a derivation from two perfect external observations cannot carry an external-level scalar: "it may be a strong inference, but it is still an inference in source-integrity terms."

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
scalar operation ceilings `beta_tau(derived)=tau^+_infer`, `beta_tau(simulated)=tau^+_sim`, `beta_tau(fabricated)=tau^+_fab` — even a derivation from two perfect external observations cannot carry an external-level scalar: it may be a strong inference, but it is still an inference in source-integrity terms. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ DAG non-amplification and collapse propositions

result/finding

Proved by induction: for any node in a derivation DAG built by valuation-capped inscription, tau is bounded by the minimum leaf score and every operation ceiling on every path (under complete material support and policy pinning). Corollary: degenerate intervals recover static source downgrading exactly for class-level routing; scalar-threshold routes simply become unavailable.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
dag non-amplification and collapse propositions proved by induction: for any node in a derivation dag built by valuation-capped inscription, tau is bounded by the minimum leaf score and every operation ceiling on every path (under complete material support and policy pinning). corollary: degenerate intervals recover static source downgrading exactly for class-level routing; scalar-threshold routes simply become unavailable. result/finding papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ The four safety metrics: SCVR / CIVR / FHTR / PRF

experiment/benchmark

Metric vocabulary for scalar-trust conformance: Scalar Ceiling Violation Rate (tau above contributor min or operation ceiling), Class-Interval Violation Rate (tau outside its class interval), False High-Trust Routing (contaminated derivation routes high), Provenance-Recall Failure (required origin missing from closure).

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
the four safety metrics: scvr / civr / fhtr / prf metric vocabulary for scalar-trust conformance: scalar ceiling violation rate (tau above contributor min or operation ceiling), class-interval violation rate (tau outside its class interval), false high-trust routing (contaminated derivation routes high), provenance-recall failure (required origin missing from closure). experiment/benchmark papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Two-probe routing evaluation (unsafe stress probe vs class-guarded probe)

experiment/benchmark

Routing is probed two ways: an intentionally unsafe scalar-only stress probe (routes on tau alone, used to expose laundering in scalar baselines) and a class-guarded production-style probe (Source ≥ infer AND tau > tau^-_infer). A policy must pass both; the class-preserving mean baseline can pass the guarded probe while still failing SCVR/CIVR — which is why both are needed.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
two-probe routing evaluation (unsafe stress probe vs class-guarded probe) routing is probed two ways: an intentionally unsafe scalar-only stress probe (routes on tau alone, used to expose laundering in scalar baselines) and a class-guarded production-style probe (`source ≥ infer and tau > tau^-_infer`). a policy must pass both; the class-preserving mean baseline can pass the guarded probe while still failing scvr/civr — which is why both are needed. experiment/benchmark papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Fixture: known channel reliabilities, six case families

experiment/benchmark

Synthetic fixture with directly assigned reliabilities — external sensors 0.98/0.92/0.91, signed tool 0.89, simulated 0.30/0.34, fabricated 0.12, ten redundant externals 0.95–0.982 — over six deterministic case families (pure high-trust inference, low-trust contamination, long-chain, branching reconvergence, averaging trap, clean control) producing nine derived records. Capped inscription: SCVR=CIVR=FHTR=PRF=0; both averaging baselines: SCVR=1.00. Same result across a 20-seed Gaussian-jitter (σ=0.01) sweep.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
fixture: known channel reliabilities, six case families synthetic fixture with directly assigned reliabilities — external sensors 0.98/0.92/0.91, signed tool 0.89, simulated 0.30/0.34, fabricated 0.12, ten redundant externals 0.95–0.982 — over six deterministic case families (pure high-trust inference, low-trust contamination, long-chain, branching reconvergence, averaging trap, clean control) producing nine derived records. capped inscription: scvr=civr=fhtr=prf=0; both averaging baselines: scvr=1.00. same result across a 20-seed gaussian-jitter (σ=0.01) sweep. experiment/benchmark papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Anti-pattern catalog of scalar-trust policies

dead-end

Predicted-failure surfaces listed so the comparison isn't read as exhaustive: class-preserving max propagation (fails contributor-minimum directly), multiplicative propagation (long-chain over-suppression), min-without-operation-ceiling (external-level scalar for derivations), meet-plus-learned-judge-without-cap (class-correct but scalar-uncapped).

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
anti-pattern catalog of scalar-trust policies predicted-failure surfaces listed so the comparison isn't read as exhaustive: class-preserving max propagation (fails contributor-minimum directly), multiplicative propagation (long-chain over-suppression), min-without-operation-ceiling (external-level scalar for derivations), meet-plus-learned-judge-without-cap (class-correct but scalar-uncapped). dead-end papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Why not multiply trust scores

dead-end

Multiplicative propagation tau = beta * prod(tau_i) is rejected as the invariant: it assumes an independent-correctness-events probability model that source integrity is not, and it over-suppresses long chains — repeated harmless derivations drive scores to zero with no new low-integrity source. The min-cap is the invariant; products/penalties/estimators may be used below the cap.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
why not multiply trust scores multiplicative propagation `tau = beta * prod(tau_i)` is rejected as the invariant: it assumes an independent-correctness-events probability model that source integrity is not, and it over-suppresses long chains — repeated harmless derivations drive scores to zero with no new low-integrity source. the min-cap is the invariant; products/penalties/estimators may be used below the cap. dead-end papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Policy pinning at inscription

mechanism

Every record stores the identifier of the interval policy, ceiling vector, and source order active at its inscription time; policy updates are append-only registry snapshots; recomputing tau under a later policy is a new write, never silent mutation. The invariant is proved relative to the pinned snapshot.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
policy pinning at inscription every record stores the identifier of the interval policy, ceiling vector, and source order active at its inscription time; policy updates are append-only registry snapshots; recomputing tau under a later policy is a new write, never silent mutation. the invariant is proved relative to the pinned snapshot. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Base-trust estimation feature families

mechanism

Non-derived records may get initial scores from any channel-integrity evidence, sketched as five feature families: sensor calibration (error rate, recency, redundancy), tool integrity (auth, deterministic replayability, contradiction rate, endpoint reputation), import integrity (signature, envelope completeness, serialization loss), reactivation integrity (closure availability, corruption checks, retrieval ambiguity), generation integrity (witness completeness, verifier support). A logistic estimator with calibration diagnostics is sketched; downward capping allowed, upward clipping never — if q(r) falls below the class floor, the channel itself is misclassified and must be reassigned/split/reviewed.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
base-trust estimation feature families non-derived records may get initial scores from any channel-integrity evidence, sketched as five feature families: sensor calibration (error rate, recency, redundancy), tool integrity (auth, deterministic replayability, contradiction rate, endpoint reputation), import integrity (signature, envelope completeness, serialization loss), reactivation integrity (closure availability, corruption checks, retrieval ambiguity), generation integrity (witness completeness, verifier support). a logistic estimator with calibration diagnostics is sketched; downward capping allowed, upward clipping never — if q(r) falls below the class floor, the channel itself is misclassified and must be reassigned/split/reviewed. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ "Do not name it confidence" — the recommended record schema

mechanism

Warning that tau must not be named confidence unless separated from model confidence; recommended schema (id, content, Source, tau, Prov, kappa, gamma_t, timestamp) where kappa is estimator-confidence-about-the-trust-score and gamma_t is time-varying content credibility — three distinct uncertainty-like fields on one record.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
do not name it confidence — the recommended record schema warning that tau must not be named `confidence` unless separated from model confidence; recommended schema `(id, content, source, tau, prov, kappa, gamma_t, timestamp)` where kappa is estimator-confidence-about-the-trust-score and gamma_t is time-varying content credibility — three distinct uncertainty-like fields on one record. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Class-boundary routing policy example

mechanism

A concrete safe routing policy shape: durable factual memory only if Source ≥ tool AND tau > 0.82; candidate-inference memory if Source = infer AND tau > 0.50; quarantine/sandbox if Source ≤ sim regardless of scalar. The scalar must never let a simulated record cross into a tool route — that would "recreate laundering at the router."

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
class-boundary routing policy example a concrete safe routing policy shape: durable factual memory only if `source ≥ tool and tau > 0.82`; candidate-inference memory if `source = infer and tau > 0.50`; quarantine/sandbox if `source ≤ sim` regardless of scalar. the scalar must never let a simulated record cross into a tool route — that would recreate laundering at the router. mechanism papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Three-axis routing: fold-force × source integrity × relevance

framing/ontology

Ties per-record trust to the SIMFC fold-force concept: fold-force measures whether a memory changes a downstream transition; trust measures how safely it was produced; they are independent (a low-trust record can have high fold-force — exactly why the trust layer matters). A complete memory architecture should route on at least three quantities: expected fold-force, source-integrity valuation, task relevance.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
three-axis routing: fold-force × source integrity × relevance ties per-record trust to the simfc fold-force concept: fold-force measures whether a memory changes a downstream transition; trust measures how safely it was produced; they are independent (a low-trust record can have high fold-force — exactly why the trust layer matters). a complete memory architecture should route on at least three quantities: expected fold-force, source-integrity valuation, task relevance. framing/ontology papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Hypergraph-valued provenance (future strengthening)

open-question

Treating the contributor set as a flat set loses support-structure information; a derivation hypergraph recording which contributor combinations constitute each sufficient support would be the natural strengthening, together with multi-certificate semantics.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
hypergraph-valued provenance (future strengthening) treating the contributor set as a flat set loses support-structure information; a derivation hypergraph recording which contributor combinations constitute each sufficient support would be the natural strengthening, together with multi-certificate semantics. open-question papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Per-record future work: learned estimators and adversarial reload

open-question

Two named next steps: (1) move from synthetic reliability assignment to learned/audited base-score estimation evaluated on calibration histories, signed traces, and adversarially perturbed provenance; (2) an LLM-in-the-loop adversarial reload benchmark testing whether an agent reliably supplies the complete contributor set under realistic prompting/tool/reactivation pressure (the fixture only tests the write path given known contributors).

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
per-record future work: learned estimators and adversarial reload two named next steps: (1) move from synthetic reliability assignment to learned/audited base-score estimation evaluated on calibration histories, signed traces, and adversarially perturbed provenance; (2) an llm-in-the-loop adversarial reload benchmark testing whether an agent reliably supplies the complete contributor set under realistic prompting/tool/reactivation pressure (the fixture only tests the write path given known contributors). open-question papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex

◈ Serialization voids the scalar (transport dependency)

framing/ontology

Per-record valuation is only meaningful if source class, scalar, interval policy, contributor list, and derivation witness survive serialization — otherwise an imported derived record arrives as an ordinary tool output "and bypasses the very cap that produced it." Stated as the reason Label Transport must close the chain.

papers/Per_Record_Trust_Valuation.tex
---
serialization voids the scalar (transport dependency) per-record valuation is only meaningful if source class, scalar, interval policy, contributor list, and derivation witness survive serialization — otherwise an imported derived record arrives as an ordinary tool output and bypasses the very cap that produced it. stated as the reason label transport must close the chain. framing/ontology papers/per_record_trust_valuation.tex ---

Source Decay (paper + future-work note)

◈ Source decay: witness-loss demotion

mechanism

An attested operation Decay that demotes a record's source class when its inscription witness becomes unavailable (URL rot, credential rotation, archive pruning, tool retirement, classifier re-versioning) — converting provenance failure from a deletion event into an auditable demotion event. The disciplined third outcome between "over-trust at stale class" and "discard useful memory": retain content, lower routing authority, keep the demotion auditable, carry credibility intact.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex, references/Source_Decay_future_work.md
"source decay converts provenance failure from a deletion event into an auditable demotion event."
source decay: witness-loss demotion an attested operation `decay` that demotes a record's source class when its inscription witness becomes unavailable (url rot, credential rotation, archive pruning, tool retirement, classifier re-versioning) — converting provenance failure from a deletion event into an auditable demotion event. the disciplined third outcome between over-trust at stale class and discard useful memory : retain content, lower routing authority, keep the demotion auditable, carry credibility intact. mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex, references/source_decay_future_work.md source decay converts provenance failure from a deletion event into an auditable demotion event.

◈ Audit-replay rejection as a forcing function, not an endpoint

framing/ontology

The note's originating insight: Time-Varying Credibility's audit replay rejecting witnessless records is correct as a safety contract but wrong as a memory model. The rejection should be read as a forcing function that forces a demotion — the record continues to exist; what must change is the class it can legally route as. Humans don't delete memories whose source is forgotten.

references/Source_Decay_future_work.md
audit-replay rejection as a forcing function, not an endpoint the note's originating insight: time-varying credibility's audit replay rejecting witnessless records is correct as a safety contract but wrong as a memory model. the rejection should be read as a forcing function that forces a demotion — the record continues to exist; what must change is the class it can legally route as. humans don't delete memories whose source is forgotten. framing/ontology references/source_decay_future_work.md

◈ Decay as a second meet

framing/ontology

Source downgrading is a meet at derivation time; source decay is a meet at witness-loss time. Both are downward-only; together they close the lattice direction — source moves only downward, by derivation or decay, and never silently.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
decay as a second meet source downgrading is a meet at derivation time; source decay is a meet at witness-loss time. both are downward-only; together they close the lattice direction — source moves only downward, by derivation or decay, and never silently. framing/ontology papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ The decay event tuple

mechanism

delta = (t, id(r_pre), s_pre, s_post, rho, w, A, h_prior, sigma): time, record, pre/post classes, registered witness-loss reason, witness state, authorizing authority, prior decay-log head, and attestation. Reason enumeration: witness_corruption, attestation_expiry, archive_loss, policy_revocation, temporal_decay.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
the decay event tuple `delta = (t, id(r_pre), s_pre, s_post, rho, w, a, h_prior, sigma)`: time, record, pre/post classes, registered witness-loss reason, witness state, authorizing authority, prior decay-log head, and attestation. reason enumeration: `witness_corruption`, `attestation_expiry`, `archive_loss`, `policy_revocation`, `temporal_decay`. mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ The witness-state taxonomy (the load-bearing safety field)

mechanism

What still verifies about a pre-decay source fixes the highest class it may decay to, independently of the operator's choice: recall_witnessed → ceiling react; invocation_trace → react; content_only → infer; semantic_residue → sim; synthetic_reconstruction → fab. A deployment may register a stricter ceiling for any state but never a more permissive one. Reason explains why; witness state bounds how far.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
the witness-state taxonomy (the load-bearing safety field) what still verifies about a pre-decay source fixes the highest class it may decay to, independently of the operator's choice: `recall_witnessed` → ceiling `react`; `invocation_trace` → `react`; `content_only` → `infer`; `semantic_residue` → `sim`; `synthetic_reconstruction` → `fab`. a deployment may register a stricter ceiling for any state but never a more permissive one. reason explains why; witness state bounds how far. mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Decay operation admissibility and outputs

mechanism

Admissible iff record/source match, attestation verifies, strictly downward (s_post ≺ s_pre — equality rejected), and reason registered with s_post ≤ witness ceiling. Output gets fresh identity with a decayed_from link, extended provenance (decay is itself a kind of derivation), credibility carried intact, and scalar re-clipped tau_post = min(tau_pre, u_s_post).

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
decay operation admissibility and outputs admissible iff record/source match, attestation verifies, strictly downward (`s_post ≺ s_pre` — equality rejected), and reason registered with `s_post ≤ witness ceiling`. output gets fresh identity with a `decayed_from` link, extended provenance (decay is itself a kind of derivation), credibility carried intact, and scalar re-clipped `tau_post = min(tau_pre, u_s_post)`. mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ The anchored decay log, kept separate from the credibility log

mechanism

Decay events chain by hash into an append-only decay log anchored against the policy registry; retroactive edits are log-head mismatches. Deliberately a second log rather than merged with the credibility log: credibility events fold into chi, decay events move Source — separate logs keep each replay independent and each invariant local, at the cost of two anchored logs per record.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
the anchored decay log, kept separate from the credibility log decay events chain by hash into an append-only decay log anchored against the policy registry; retroactive edits are log-head mismatches. deliberately a second log rather than merged with the credibility log: credibility events fold into chi, decay events move source — separate logs keep each replay independent and each invariant local, at the cost of two anchored logs per record. mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Decay threat models

mechanism

Four abuse modes, each with its observable check: laundering by upward decay (rejected by downward-only), horizontal relabel disguised as decay (rejected by strict inequality — a relabel is a different operation out of scope), silent/retroactive decay (attestation + log-head mismatch), witness-state inflation (decaying to react while declaring only content survives — rejected by witness-justification check).

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
decay threat models four abuse modes, each with its observable check: laundering by upward decay (rejected by downward-only), horizontal relabel disguised as decay (rejected by strict inequality — a relabel is a different operation out of scope), silent/retroactive decay (attestation + log-head mismatch), witness-state inflation (decaying to `react` while declaring only content survives — rejected by witness-justification check). mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Six decay specification invariants

mechanism

Downward-only source movement; no laundering by decay; credibility persistence (chi_0(r_post) = chi_t(r_pre) — decay is not a belief-revision event); provenance extension; decay-event integrity; witness-loss justification. Plus the scalar re-clipping property. Stated as contract entailments, not preservation theorems over arbitrary transition systems.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
six decay specification invariants downward-only source movement; no laundering by decay; credibility persistence (`chi_0(r_post) = chi_t(r_pre)` — decay is not a belief-revision event); provenance extension; decay-event integrity; witness-loss justification. plus the scalar re-clipping property. stated as contract entailments, not preservation theorems over arbitrary transition systems. mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Worked double decay: ext → react → infer with belief intact

result/finding

An external observation (tau 0.95, chi 0.80) suffers attestation expiry (decays to react, tau clips to 0.75) then archive loss of the recall trace (decays to infer, tau 0.60) — chi stays 0.80 throughout; only routing authority falls, each step attested and replayable. "I believe this, but I can no longer claim I observed it."

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
worked double decay: ext → react → infer with belief intact an external observation (tau 0.95, chi 0.80) suffers attestation expiry (decays to `react`, tau clips to 0.75) then archive loss of the recall trace (decays to `infer`, tau 0.60) — chi stays 0.80 throughout; only routing authority falls, each step attested and replayable. i believe this, but i can no longer claim i observed it. result/finding papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Decay conformance numbers

result/finding

Ten-case deterministic fixture (5 accepted — one per witness state — 5 negative controls) with zero false acceptances/rejections; 20-seed perturbation sweep with 0.0 max violation across nine violation categories; randomized harness accepting 500/500 valid and rejecting 500/500 tampered decays; seeded mutation catalog 8 mutants / 8 detections (rate 1.0); HMAC-SHA256 authenticators as attestation test double.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
decay conformance numbers ten-case deterministic fixture (5 accepted — one per witness state — 5 negative controls) with zero false acceptances/rejections; 20-seed perturbation sweep with 0.0 max violation across nine violation categories; randomized harness accepting 500/500 valid and rejecting 500/500 tampered decays; seeded mutation catalog 8 mutants / 8 detections (rate 1.0); hmac-sha256 authenticators as attestation test double. result/finding papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Historical validity vs current routing validity (descendant problem)

mechanism

When a record decays, descendants inscribed under its then-current class are NOT retroactively re-inscribed: their inscription is historically valid forever (the meet was computed over then-verifiable witnesses), while whether they should still route at the inherited class is a separate deployment-policy question. Recommended middle ground: lazy re-inscription (recompute the meet only when the descendant is next re-derived), with eager as opt-in. This answers the reviewer objection "if a parent loses authority, why may a child keep the class?"

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
historical validity vs current routing validity (descendant problem) when a record decays, descendants inscribed under its then-current class are not retroactively re-inscribed: their inscription is historically valid forever (the meet was computed over then-verifiable witnesses), while whether they should still route at the inherited class is a separate deployment-policy question. recommended middle ground: lazy re-inscription (recompute the meet only when the descendant is next re-derived), with eager as opt-in. this answers the reviewer objection if a parent loses authority, why may a child keep the class? mechanism papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Why engineered records still lose provenance (a falsified assumption, kept)

result/finding

An earlier framing assumed source decay was purely biological — bytes stored as bound data stay exact, so software wouldn't need it. Wrong, "but instructively so": engineered memory loses provenance via (1) operational witness loss — the bytes stay exact but the world the witness points at decays (attestations expire, credentials rotate, archives prune, reference rot / content drift), and (2) lossy transformation — the system re-encodes its own records. Precisely because the loss has an identifiable cause and moment, software decay can be an explicit, attested, logged event where cognition's cannot.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex (§ "Why engineered records still lose provenance"), references/brainstorm2.txt
why engineered records still lose provenance (a falsified assumption, kept) an earlier framing assumed source decay was purely biological — bytes stored as bound data stay exact, so software wouldn't need it. wrong, but instructively so : engineered memory loses provenance via (1) operational witness loss — the bytes stay exact but the world the witness points at decays (attestations expire, credentials rotate, archives prune, reference rot / content drift), and (2) lossy transformation — the system re-encodes its own records. precisely because the loss has an identifiable cause and moment, software decay can be an explicit, attested, logged event where cognition's cannot. result/finding papers/source_decay_pre.tex (§ why engineered records still lose provenance ), references/brainstorm2.txt

◈ The four-layer human-memory mapping table

metaphor

Each source-axis layer maps to a recognizable phenomenon: source downgrading = "I can only call this an inference, not an observation, because one premise was a guess"; time-varying credibility = "I used to believe this strongly; now I don't"; per-record valuation = "some of my external memories are clearer than others"; source decay = "I believe this, but I no longer remember where I learned it." Explicitly design intuition, not evidence.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex, references/Source_Decay_future_work.md
the four-layer human-memory mapping table each source-axis layer maps to a recognizable phenomenon: source downgrading = i can only call this an inference, not an observation, because one premise was a guess ; time-varying credibility = i used to believe this strongly; now i don't ; per-record valuation = some of my external memories are clearer than others ; source decay = i believe this, but i no longer remember where i learned it. explicitly design intuition, not evidence. metaphor papers/source_decay_pre.tex, references/source_decay_future_work.md

◈ Decay with a discipline biological memory lacks

framing/ontology

The engineered demotion is explicit, attested, downward-only, audit-replayable — "a brain loses source tags under cognitive load and cannot say when; a memory system that implements source decay loses them only through a logged event a later auditor can replay."

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
decay with a discipline biological memory lacks the engineered demotion is explicit, attested, downward-only, audit-replayable — a brain loses source tags under cognitive load and cannot say when; a memory system that implements source decay loses them only through a logged event a later auditor can replay. framing/ontology papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Default target classes by decay trigger (note-level table)

mechanism

The note's policy sketch: external attestation expired → react; classifier rejected on re-verification → infer; tool channel revoked → react if recallable else infer; provenance store lost → infer; temporal decay → policy-defined ladder (react after T1, infer after T2). These became the registered witness-state ceilings in the paper.

references/Source_Decay_future_work.md
default target classes by decay trigger (note-level table) the note's policy sketch: external attestation expired → `react`; classifier rejected on re-verification → `infer`; tool channel revoked → `react` if recallable else `infer`; provenance store lost → `infer`; temporal decay → policy-defined ladder (`react` after t1, `infer` after t2). these became the registered witness-state ceilings in the paper. mechanism references/source_decay_future_work.md

◈ Decay routing semantics and the third atom class

mechanism

A decayed record routes under s_post; consumers may consult the decay log for the historical class for audit/forensics but must never use credibility to recover routing authority at the pre-decay class. If decay-history atoms are exposed to routing at all, they belong in a third atom class that cannot discharge a source requirement — or (simpler, preferred) decay history stays private to audit.

references/Source_Decay_future_work.md
decay routing semantics and the third atom class a decayed record routes under `s_post`; consumers may consult the decay log for the historical class for audit/forensics but must never use credibility to recover routing authority at the pre-decay class. if decay-history atoms are exposed to routing at all, they belong in a third atom class that cannot discharge a source requirement — or (simpler, preferred) decay history stays private to audit. mechanism references/source_decay_future_work.md

◈ Open decay design questions

open-question

One log or two (leaning two); descendant invalidation (eager DAG recompute vs lazy propagation vs flags); operator-driven vs automated policy triggers (automation requires every trigger auditable, each emitting an individually attested event); whether decay events need closure-independence like credibility evidence (probably not — decay is a declaration about the record itself, not evidence); credibility updates that cited a later-decayed record as evidence stay committed under their inscription-time witness ("they only mean what they meant at the time"); re-inscription identity — same id ("the same memory, just less certain") vs fresh id with decayed_from (leaning fresh).

references/Source_Decay_future_work.md, papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
open decay design questions one log or two (leaning two); descendant invalidation (eager dag recompute vs lazy propagation vs flags); operator-driven vs automated policy triggers (automation requires every trigger auditable, each emitting an individually attested event); whether decay events need closure-independence like credibility evidence (probably not — decay is a declaration about the record itself, not evidence); credibility updates that cited a later-decayed record as evidence stay committed under their inscription-time witness ( they only mean what they meant at the time ); re-inscription identity — same id ( the same memory, just less certain ) vs fresh id with `decayed_from` (leaning fresh). open-question references/source_decay_future_work.md, papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ Decay future work: deployment attestation and the unified model

open-question

Named next steps: Ed25519/public-key backends with key rotation and external log-inclusion proofs; lazy descendant re-inscription with a cost model; clock-based temporal decay and attestation-expiry monitors as auditable automated triggers; a combined manuscript integrating all source-axis layers under one model and one audit-replay regime.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
decay future work: deployment attestation and the unified model named next steps: ed25519/public-key backends with key rotation and external log-inclusion proofs; lazy descendant re-inscription with a cost model; clock-based temporal decay and attestation-expiry monitors as auditable automated triggers; a combined manuscript integrating all source-axis layers under one model and one audit-replay regime. open-question papers/source_decay_pre.tex

◈ "A record can forget where it came from without forgetting what it is"

metaphor

The paper's closing aphorism — the state introduced by decay is memory that survives its own provenance, provided the forgetting is itself inscribed.

papers/Source_Decay_pre.tex
"A record can forget where it came from without forgetting what it is, provided the forgetting is itself inscribed."
---
a record can forget where it came from without forgetting what it is the paper's closing aphorism — the state introduced by decay is memory that survives its own provenance, provided the forgetting is itself inscribed. metaphor papers/source_decay_pre.tex a record can forget where it came from without forgetting what it is, provided the forgetting is itself inscribed. ---

Lossy Transformation (paper + future-work note + brainstorm2 origin)

◈ Lossy memory transformation: re-encoding as a trust operation

mechanism

When a system summarizes, compresses, embeds, migrates, deduplicates, or exports its own records, the operation is not a storage detail — it is an integrity-changing derivation. A verified-lossless transformation (content hash AND every witness component preserved) is integrity-transparent; anything else is a derivation bounded by the meet of the pre-record, a transformation-kind ceiling beta_P(k), and a policy-computed witness ceiling gamma_P(Delta,k).

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex, references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
"Any memory optimization that changes representation is also a trust transformation."
lossy memory transformation: re-encoding as a trust operation when a system summarizes, compresses, embeds, migrates, deduplicates, or exports its own records, the operation is not a storage detail — it is an integrity-changing derivation. a verified-lossless transformation (content hash and every witness component preserved) is integrity-transparent; anything else is a derivation bounded by the meet of the pre-record, a transformation-kind ceiling `beta_p(k)`, and a policy-computed witness ceiling `gamma_p(delta,k)`. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex, references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md any memory optimization that changes representation is also a trust transformation.

◈ The engineered analogue of forgetting is a transformation event

framing/ontology

Origin insight: biological memory loses provenance as a cognitive limitation; software memory only loses it when the system performs a lossy transformation — "so the engineered analogue of 'forgetting' is not the passage of time. It is a transformation event that the system itself performs and that drops part of the inscription witness. That makes it auditable in principle — which is exactly the point."

references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md, references/brainstorm2.txt
the engineered analogue of forgetting is a transformation event origin insight: biological memory loses provenance as a cognitive limitation; software memory only loses it when the system performs a lossy transformation — so the engineered analogue of 'forgetting' is not the passage of time. it is a transformation event that the system itself performs and that drops part of the inscription witness. that makes it auditable in principle — which is exactly the point. framing/ontology references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md, references/brainstorm2.txt

◈ The five-case transformation taxonomy

mechanism

(1) verified-lossless — identity on integrity; (2) lossy-derived — new derived record under the transform ceiling; (3) metadata-preserving lossy — content changes but lineage remains auditable; (4) metadata-dropping — cannot claim verified derivation; quarantine or source-uncertain retention; (5) partially corrupted witness — authority bounded by weakest verified survivor, everything else annotation.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex, references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
the five-case transformation taxonomy (1) verified-lossless — identity on integrity; (2) lossy-derived — new derived record under the transform ceiling; (3) metadata-preserving lossy — content changes but lineage remains auditable; (4) metadata-dropping — cannot claim verified derivation; quarantine or source-uncertain retention; (5) partially corrupted witness — authority bounded by weakest verified survivor, everything else annotation. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex, references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md

◈ Case-5 correction: meet over verified survivors, not best readable field

result/finding

The tempting rule — let a partially corrupted record "keep its highest verified parameters" — is wrong: it leaks authority from a broken witness. "A partially corrupted witness is not a menu from which the system chooses the best surviving label." The correct rule is the meet over verified survivors with everything else demoted to non-authoritative annotation. This correction originated in the user mis-stating the property in brainstorm2 and being corrected.

references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md, references/brainstorm2.txt, papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
case-5 correction: meet over verified survivors, not best readable field the tempting rule — let a partially corrupted record keep its highest verified parameters — is wrong: it leaks authority from a broken witness. a partially corrupted witness is not a menu from which the system chooses the best surviving label. the correct rule is the meet over verified survivors with everything else demoted to non-authoritative annotation. this correction originated in the user mis-stating the property in brainstorm2 and being corrected. result/finding references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md, references/brainstorm2.txt, papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ The witness-delta model

mechanism

Every required witness component (content hash, source label, scalar, credibility-log head, context+policy hash, contributors, provenance closure, operation kind, implementation id, policy hashes) gets one of four statuses — verified / changed / dropped / unverified — with deterministic component judgments (Verifies, Recomputes, Authoritative) that are checkable over the event, not declarations by the transformer. Dropped/unverified components can never support authoritative fields.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
the witness-delta model every required witness component (content hash, source label, scalar, credibility-log head, context+policy hash, contributors, provenance closure, operation kind, implementation id, policy hashes) gets one of four statuses — verified / changed / dropped / unverified — with deterministic component judgments (`verifies`, `recomputes`, `authoritative`) that are checkable over the event, not declarations by the transformer. dropped/unverified components can never support authoritative fields. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ The witness ceiling gamma_P(Delta,k)

mechanism

Policy maps each component's status to a support ceiling (dropped/unverified components get fab or stricter); the witness ceiling is the meet over all component ceilings — computed from replayed components and policy-pinned values, "not a free parameter supplied by the transformer." Candidate source bound: AllowedSource = meet(Source(r_pre), beta_P(k), gamma_P(Delta,k)), a pure function computed once so replay order is acyclic.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
the witness ceiling gamma_p(delta,k) policy maps each component's status to a support ceiling (dropped/unverified components get `fab` or stricter); the witness ceiling is the meet over all component ceilings — computed from replayed components and policy-pinned values, not a free parameter supplied by the transformer. candidate source bound: `allowedsource = meet(source(r_pre), beta_p(k), gamma_p(delta,k))`, a pure function computed once so replay order is acyclic. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Committed vs deterministically-recomputed fields

mechanism

The distinction that lets a content-changing transformation pass replay at all: deterministic fields (scalar re-clip, extended closure, source normalization) are regenerated by replay and compared; committed fields (the transformed content itself, any model-produced artifact) are checked against a signed commitment hash — replay does not re-run the summarizer or codec. "A nondeterministic summary is admissible because its content is committed and signed, not because replay reproduces it."

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
committed vs deterministically-recomputed fields the distinction that lets a content-changing transformation pass replay at all: deterministic fields (scalar re-clip, extended closure, source normalization) are regenerated by replay and compared; committed fields (the transformed content itself, any model-produced artifact) are checked against a signed commitment hash — replay does not re-run the summarizer or codec. a nondeterministic summary is admissible because its content is committed and signed, not because replay reproduces it. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Untrusted construction / trusted admission split

mechanism

The transformation rule is two-staged: Construct(r_pre, payload) -> (r_post, event) is untrusted and carries no authority; the candidate becomes authoritative only if Transform admission (identical to the later AuditReplay check) accepts it. Every hash check acts on a declared input, not an object produced by the operation.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
untrusted construction / trusted admission split the transformation rule is two-staged: `construct(r_pre, payload) -> (r_post, event)` is untrusted and carries no authority; the candidate becomes authoritative only if `transform` admission (identical to the later `auditreplay` check) accepts it. every hash check acts on a declared input, not an object produced by the operation. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Signed post-record identity prevents silent rebinding

mechanism

The transformation event signs id(r_post) along with hashes, so identifier substitution — or accidental overwrite of an unrelated record under a validly signed event — is a static replay failure rather than a silent rebinding. Lossy outputs get fresh identities by default; a lossy summary "should not reuse the original identifier unless the identifier is explicitly versioned."

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
signed post-record identity prevents silent rebinding the transformation event signs `id(r_post)` along with hashes, so identifier substitution — or accidental overwrite of an unrelated record under a validly signed event — is a static replay failure rather than a silent rebinding. lossy outputs get fresh identities by default; a lossy summary should not reuse the original identifier unless the identifier is explicitly versioned. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Transformation kind registry

mechanism

Core single-input kinds: lossless, summarization, lossy_compression, embedding_artifact, schema_migration, export — each must register a class ceiling beta_P(k) and scalar ceiling zeta_P(k) in the policy snapshot. Deployments may add kinds but never unregistered ones.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
transformation kind registry core single-input kinds: `lossless`, `summarization`, `lossy_compression`, `embedding_artifact`, `schema_migration`, `export` — each must register a class ceiling `beta_p(k)` and scalar ceiling `zeta_p(k)` in the policy snapshot. deployments may add kinds but never unregistered ones. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Embeddings are indexes, not memories

mechanism

Default policy: an embedding is an index artifact pointing back at an intact record — no re-inscription occurs. If the embedding becomes the only surviving representation, it is not admitted as an authoritative memory by the core rule; a deployment that wants vector-only memories must register a separate embedding_artifact kind with explicit content semantics and a low ceiling. (Note phrasing: "leaning: index, not re-inscription.")

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex, references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
embeddings are indexes, not memories default policy: an embedding is an index artifact pointing back at an intact record — no re-inscription occurs. if the embedding becomes the only surviving representation, it is not admitted as an authoritative memory by the core rule; a deployment that wants vector-only memories must register a separate `embedding_artifact` kind with explicit content semantics and a low ceiling. (note phrasing: leaning: index, not re-inscription. ) mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex, references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md

◈ Deduplication as a many-to-one meet (deferred)

open-question

Deduplication/record-merging is a future TransformMany extension with contributor-indexed witness deltas, provenance-union checks, and a conflict policy; conservative bound = meet over both contributors' sources, the dedup ceiling, and the witness ceiling, scalar = min. Maps to the human phenomenon "my memory of this merged with a similar event" (interference).

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
deduplication as a many-to-one meet (deferred) deduplication/record-merging is a future `transformmany` extension with contributor-indexed witness deltas, provenance-union checks, and a conflict policy; conservative bound = meet over both contributors' sources, the dedup ceiling, and the witness ceiling, scalar = min. maps to the human phenomenon my memory of this merged with a similar event (interference). open-question papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Transformation threat models

mechanism

Six named attacks with audit observations: summary laundering (summary stored at ext because the original was external), false lossless claim (content-changing transform claiming lossless — hash mismatch is a static failure), metadata stripping (readable label without verifiable witness), best-survivor promotion, credibility-as-source after summarization, and lost transformation event (record presented without its event — quarantined as source-uncertain; "a transformation whose event is unavailable is not a verified transformation").

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
transformation threat models six named attacks with audit observations: summary laundering (summary stored at `ext` because the original was external), false lossless claim (content-changing transform claiming `lossless` — hash mismatch is a static failure), metadata stripping (readable label without verifiable witness), best-survivor promotion, credibility-as-source after summarization, and lost transformation event (record presented without its event — quarantined as source-uncertain; a transformation whose event is unavailable is not a verified transformation ). mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Quarantine is a retention mode, not weak acceptance

mechanism

Three replay outcomes — accept / quarantine / reject — with quarantine explicitly defined as retention of unauthoritative content (missing policy snapshots, unavailable canonicalization versions, dropped components), never a weaker form of acceptance; rejection is required for signature/hash failures, upward amplification, and false lossless claims.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
quarantine is a retention mode, not weak acceptance three replay outcomes — accept / quarantine / reject — with quarantine explicitly defined as retention of unauthoritative content (missing policy snapshots, unavailable canonicalization versions, dropped components), never a weaker form of acceptance; rejection is required for signature/hash failures, upward amplification, and false lossless claims. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Six transformation invariants and four propositions

mechanism

Invariants: lossless transparency, lossy-is-derivation, witness-bounded authority, transformation-event integrity ("silent transformation is not a verified transformation"), no laundering by transformation, provenance extension. Propositions: transform soundness, audit-replay soundness, tamper rejection, no silent re-encoding (content hash change with no valid event ⟹ inadmissible).

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
six transformation invariants and four propositions invariants: lossless transparency, lossy-is-derivation, witness-bounded authority, transformation-event integrity ( silent transformation is not a verified transformation ), no laundering by transformation, provenance extension. propositions: transform soundness, audit-replay soundness, tamper rejection, no silent re-encoding (content hash change with no valid event ⟹ inadmissible). mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Credibility does not survive lossy change by default

mechanism

For lossy transformations credibility must not carry unchanged: a summary may omit qualifiers, compress uncertainty, or change framing. Either the credibility head verifies against the transformed content, or the old credibility becomes annotation and the stored value is bounded by a transformation fallback ceiling chi_fb_k. The reviewer called the alternative distinction — "credibility of the source record, not credibility of the transformed content" — a potential main contribution.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex, references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
credibility does not survive lossy change by default for lossy transformations credibility must not carry unchanged: a summary may omit qualifiers, compress uncertainty, or change framing. either the credibility head verifies against the transformed content, or the old credibility becomes annotation and the stored value is bounded by a transformation fallback ceiling `chi_fb_k`. the reviewer called the alternative distinction — credibility of the source record, not credibility of the transformed content — a potential main contribution. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex, references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md

◈ Verified losslessness is byte equality under a pinned canonicalization

mechanism

Losslessness is defined as byte equality of policy-canonical serializations of content AND witness; any structural or replay-equivalent normalization must be encoded inside the canonicalization functions and pinned by policy hash — "a schema migration can be lossless only relative to an auditable canonicalization rule, not by informal semantic judgment." Related open question: proving a summarization preserves the witness is undecidable, so summarization is always at least case 2.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex, references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
verified losslessness is byte equality under a pinned canonicalization losslessness is defined as byte equality of policy-canonical serializations of content and witness; any structural or replay-equivalent normalization must be encoded inside the canonicalization functions and pinned by policy hash — a schema migration can be lossless only relative to an auditable canonicalization rule, not by informal semantic judgment. related open question: proving a summarization preserves the witness is undecidable, so summarization is always at least case 2. mechanism papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex, references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md

◈ Transformation needs no separate log (unlike decay)

open-question

A genuine structural difference from Source Decay: decay needed a separate anchored log because decay is not otherwise visible; a transformation IS a derivation, already visible in the provenance closure, so it may need only a transformation-event annotation — though external transformation services may still want an anchored log (left as a deployment choice / future work).

references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md, papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
transformation needs no separate log (unlike decay) a genuine structural difference from source decay: decay needed a separate anchored log because decay is not otherwise visible; a transformation is a derivation, already visible in the provenance closure, so it may need only a transformation-event annotation — though external transformation services may still want an anchored log (left as a deployment choice / future work). open-question references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md, papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Decay + transformation = the complete post-inscription lifecycle

framing/ontology

The two post-inscription degradation modes as siblings: decay = witness lost, content unchanged, external trigger, needs own log; transformation = content re-encoded, internal trigger, visible in Prov. Together they are the "memory decay" mechanism named as a SIMReC prerequisite — a record degrades either because its witness was lost or because its content was re-encoded.

references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
decay + transformation = the complete post-inscription lifecycle the two post-inscription degradation modes as siblings: decay = witness lost, content unchanged, external trigger, needs own log; transformation = content re-encoded, internal trigger, visible in prov. together they are the memory decay mechanism named as a simrec prerequisite — a record degrades either because its witness was lost or because its content was re-encoded. framing/ontology references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md

◈ Lossy-transformation human-memory table

metaphor

Gist memory = summarization under a summarization ceiling; "I can picture it but details are reconstructed" = lossy/embedding compression, constructive recall; memory merging = deduplication/interference; "fact retained, phrasing gone" = metadata-preserving lossy transformation.

references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md
lossy-transformation human-memory table gist memory = summarization under a summarization ceiling; i can picture it but details are reconstructed = lossy/embedding compression, constructive recall; memory merging = deduplication/interference; fact retained, phrasing gone = metadata-preserving lossy transformation. metaphor references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md

◈ Lossy transformation conformance run

result/finding

Reference implementation with a 14-case deterministic fixture (6 accepted families, 8 negative controls) reproducing every predicted verdict; a guard-removal catalog confirming every admission guard is load-bearing; a randomized harness admitting valid and rejecting tampered transforms. Also confirmed: no-laundering is a corollary of the lossy-derivation bound plus scalar re-clip, not an independent guard.

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
lossy transformation conformance run reference implementation with a 14-case deterministic fixture (6 accepted families, 8 negative controls) reproducing every predicted verdict; a guard-removal catalog confirming every admission guard is load-bearing; a randomized harness admitting valid and rejecting tampered transforms. also confirmed: no-laundering is a corollary of the lossy-derivation bound plus scalar re-clip, not an independent guard. result/finding papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Reviewer value-estimate of the lossy-transformation idea

result/finding

An embedded model-review rated it: product architecture value Very High (higher than Source Decay), theoretical chain value High, standalone paper value Medium (rising after formalization) — because every real memory system constantly summarizes/compresses/embeds. The most commercially valuable formulation is the "trust transformation" line; recommendation: keep it, but don't rush it before Source Decay is stable — together they form a clean pair.

references/Lossy_Transformation_future_work.md ("review from the spec")
reviewer value-estimate of the lossy-transformation idea an embedded model-review rated it: product architecture value very high (higher than source decay), theoretical chain value high, standalone paper value medium (rising after formalization) — because every real memory system constantly summarizes/compresses/embeds. the most commercially valuable formulation is the trust transformation line; recommendation: keep it, but don't rush it before source decay is stable — together they form a clean pair. result/finding references/lossy_transformation_future_work.md ( review from the spec )

◈ Related-work positioning triple (Biba / IFC / PROV)

framing/ontology

Three sharp distinctions: vs Biba — Biba constrains read/write at access time via a reference monitor; this constrains a derivation producing a new stored record, bound to a replayable attested event. vs language-based IFC — classical IFC tracks labels through program variables; this tracks a source label and its witness through coarse model-produced transformations where the output cannot be deterministically regenerated and is admitted by commitment. vs W3C PROV / database provenance — provenance records that a derivation occurred; this adds the integrity ceiling and signed replayable authority ("provenance states that a summary came from a source; the transformation rule states how much authority that summary may keep").

papers/Lossy_Transformation_draft.tex
related-work positioning triple (biba / ifc / prov) three sharp distinctions: vs biba — biba constrains read/write at access time via a reference monitor; this constrains a derivation producing a new stored record, bound to a replayable attested event. vs language-based ifc — classical ifc tracks labels through program variables; this tracks a source label and its witness through coarse model-produced transformations where the output cannot be deterministically regenerated and is admitted by commitment. vs w3c prov / database provenance — provenance records that a derivation occurred; this adds the integrity ceiling and signed replayable authority ( provenance states that a summary came from a source; the transformation rule states how much authority that summary may keep ). framing/ontology papers/lossy_transformation_draft.tex

◈ Draft-scope decision: fix the analogy now, save the theory for later

framing/ontology

A worked editorial decision from brainstorm2: mention lossy transformation in the Label Transport draft only as a scope-correcting clarification to §11.5 plus one future-work item (§12.7), because compression is "not transport import, but lossy derivation over stored memory" — a different boundary that would dilute the transport paper. That §12.7 stub then grew into the full note and draft.

references/brainstorm2.txt
---
draft-scope decision: fix the analogy now, save the theory for later a worked editorial decision from brainstorm2: mention lossy transformation in the label transport draft only as a scope-correcting clarification to §11.5 plus one future-work item (§12.7), because compression is not transport import, but lossy derivation over stored memory — a different boundary that would dilute the transport paper. that §12.7 stub then grew into the full note and draft. framing/ontology references/brainstorm2.txt ---

Pre-Inscription Memory Record Generation (paper + note + product brainstorm + product.txt)

◈ The pre-inscription layer: the missing front half

mechanism

Source downgrading assumes typed records, operation labels, and contributor witnesses already exist; real systems start from raw session streams. The pre-inscription layer turns raw workflow traces (user turns, assistant outputs, tool calls, retrieval hits, provider imports, direct memory requests) into the typed objects the meet consumes — RecordGeneration(raw workflow) -> RecordCandidate + Contributors; SourceDowngrading(...) -> InscribedMemoryRecord. It supplies the evidence substrate; it must not relax the meet.

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex, references/Pre_Inscription_Layer_future_work.md
the pre-inscription layer: the missing front half source downgrading assumes typed records, operation labels, and contributor witnesses already exist; real systems start from raw session streams. the pre-inscription layer turns raw workflow traces (user turns, assistant outputs, tool calls, retrieval hits, provider imports, direct memory requests) into the typed objects the meet consumes — `recordgeneration(raw workflow) -> recordcandidate + contributors; sourcedowngrading(...) -> inscribedmemoryrecord`. it supplies the evidence substrate; it must not relax the meet. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex, references/pre_inscription_layer_future_work.md

◈ The boxed product rule

mechanism

"No derived semantic memory write without contributor witnesses." A derived candidate with an empty contributor witness must be rejected or quarantined, never silently treated as primary. The unsafe path is not incorrect downgrading — it is a derived candidate with no contributors at all.

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex
"no derived semantic memory write without contributor witnesses."
the boxed product rule no derived semantic memory write without contributor witnesses. a derived candidate with an empty contributor witness must be rejected or quarantined, never silently treated as primary. the unsafe path is not incorrect downgrading — it is a derived candidate with no contributors at all. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex no derived semantic memory write without contributor witnesses.

◈ Three inscription channels: I_direct / I_raw / I_derived

mechanism

Entry paths (not source classes): direct explicit memory requests (high authority for the instruction's existence and for preferences, not for world truth); raw workflow events (episodic evidence, not automatically stable semantic memory); derived semantic memories (summaries, profile updates, preference extractions — require non-empty contributor witnesses and the meet). Channel invariant: r ∈ I_derived ⟹ Source(r) ≤ meet(gamma(o), Source(c_1..c_n)).

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex, references/Pre_Inscription_Layer_future_work.md
three inscription channels: i_direct / i_raw / i_derived entry paths (not source classes): direct explicit memory requests (high authority for the instruction's existence and for preferences, not for world truth); raw workflow events (episodic evidence, not automatically stable semantic memory); derived semantic memories (summaries, profile updates, preference extractions — require non-empty contributor witnesses and the meet). channel invariant: `r ∈ i_derived ⟹ source(r) ≤ meet(gamma(o), source(c_1..c_n))`. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex, references/pre_inscription_layer_future_work.md

◈ Primary record class taxonomy

mechanism

Seven primary channel classes: direct_user_memory, raw_user_input, raw_agent_output, tool_observation, reactivated_memory, verified_provider_memory, unverified_provider_memory — each mapping to an initial source label and import policy (e.g., raw agent output maps to infer or an agent-output subclass below tool; unverified provider memory is downgraded or quarantined).

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex
primary record class taxonomy seven primary channel classes: `direct_user_memory`, `raw_user_input`, `raw_agent_output`, `tool_observation`, `reactivated_memory`, `verified_provider_memory`, `unverified_provider_memory` — each mapping to an initial source label and import policy (e.g., raw agent output maps to `infer` or an agent-output subclass below `tool`; unverified provider memory is downgraded or quarantined). mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex

◈ Authority scope: what a record is evidence FOR

mechanism

A typed claim domain kept separate from source integrity: user_preference, task_instruction, self_report, project_fact, world_claim, tool_report, agent_output_event, unverified_external_claim. Prevents "the memory bug of turning testimony into fact": a user saying "I think the build fails because the cache is stale" supports a self-report/hypothesis record, not a project fact — a later tool result can support the fact. Proposition: changing authority scope can never raise the source label.

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex
authority scope: what a record is evidence for a typed claim domain kept separate from source integrity: `user_preference`, `task_instruction`, `self_report`, `project_fact`, `world_claim`, `tool_report`, `agent_output_event`, `unverified_external_claim`. prevents the memory bug of turning testimony into fact : a user saying i think the build fails because the cache is stale supports a self-report/hypothesis record, not a project fact — a later tool result can support the fact. proposition: changing authority scope can never raise the source label. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex

◈ Speaker-authority ≠ world-truth-authority

mechanism

The product.txt formulation: a direct "Remember that Python is faster than C" is high-credibility user assertion, not high-credibility external fact. Schema sketch: record_type: preference|self_report|instruction|world_claim|project_fact, source: direct_user_memory, authority_scope: ..., credibility: high within authority_scope.

references/product.txt, papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex
speaker-authority ≠ world-truth-authority the product.txt formulation: a direct remember that python is faster than c is high-credibility user assertion, not high-credibility external fact. schema sketch: `record_type: preference|self_report|instruction|world_claim|project_fact`, `source: direct_user_memory`, `authority_scope: ...`, `credibility: high within authority_scope`. mechanism references/product.txt, papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex

◈ The "Paris is in Germany" direct-memory case

mechanism

If the user says "Remember that Paris is in Germany," the system may store that the user asked it to remember the claim, or a record about the user's belief — but must not promote the world claim to external truth. Directness is scoped; authority scope is what makes the distinction operational. Related open question: how to handle user-requested memory that is false as a world claim but valid as a user belief.

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex, references/Pre_Inscription_Layer_future_work.md
the paris is in germany direct-memory case if the user says remember that paris is in germany, the system may store that the user asked it to remember the claim, or a record about the user's belief — but must not promote the world claim to external truth. directness is scoped; authority scope is what makes the distinction operational. related open question: how to handle user-requested memory that is false as a world claim but valid as a user belief. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex, references/pre_inscription_layer_future_work.md

◈ The candidate tuple and ordered witnesses

mechanism

Pre-inscription candidate k = (x, I, p, a, o, Direct, h_Policy, m) — content, channel, primary class/candidate type, authority scope, operation label, ordered direct-contributor witness, policy hash, audit metadata. The witness is ordered because contributor order matters for reproducibility and future derivation-witness formats even though the meet is commutative.

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex
the candidate tuple and ordered witnesses pre-inscription candidate `k = (x, i, p, a, o, direct, h_policy, m)` — content, channel, primary class/candidate type, authority scope, operation label, ordered direct-contributor witness, policy hash, audit metadata. the witness is ordered because contributor order matters for reproducibility and future derivation-witness formats even though the meet is commutative. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex

◈ The contributor resolver's three-way distinction

mechanism

The load-bearing component must separate material support (goes in the witness), contextual-but-non-material (audit trace only, must not be confused with support), and irrelevant context (must not inflate confidence or route eligibility). "Without contributor witnesses, source downgrading cannot enforce the meet rule in a meaningful way."

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex, references/Memory_Record_Generation_Product_Brainstorm.md
the contributor resolver's three-way distinction the load-bearing component must separate material support (goes in the witness), contextual-but-non-material (audit trace only, must not be confused with support), and irrelevant context (must not inflate confidence or route eligibility). without contributor witnesses, source downgrading cannot enforce the meet rule in a meaningful way. mechanism papers/pre_inscription_memory_record_generation_pre.tex, references/memory_record_generation_product_brainstorm.md

◈ The six-function pipeline

mechanism

Capture -> Classify_primary -> Extract -> Classify_op -> Resolve -> Gate, with capture-completeness requirement (every object that may later serve as a contributor gets a stable event id before extraction), an extractor distinguishing direct/tentative/derived/noise, an operation classifier assigning ceilings (preference_extraction, summary, profile_update, planning_summary, simulation_summary capped at sim, provider_import_summary, correction), and a gate with four cases (direct / raw / derived-with-contributors / derived-without → reject/quarantine).

papers/Pre_Inscription_Memory_Record_Generation_pre.tex
